name: Opus 4.6 Exam Simulation
description: 60 realistic scenario-based questions generated by Claude Opus 4.6, emulating real CCA-F exam format with 4-5 sentence production scenarios. Domain-weighted: D1 (27%), D2 (18%), D3 (20%), D4 (20%), D5 (15%). NOT affiliated with Anthropic.
disclaimer: AI-generated exam simulation by Claude Opus 4.6. These are NOT real exam questions. Generated for educational preparation purposes only — not produced, endorsed, or affiliated with Anthropic.

questions.question: Production logs show a customer support agent incorrectly routes 18% of refund requests to the `check_order_status` tool instead of `process_refund`. Both tools have one-line descriptions. What is the most effective first step to improve routing accuracy?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a separate NLP intent classifier to pre-route tool calls before the agent runs.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: NLP classifiers duplicate the agent's already-sophisticated language understanding. Adds latency and failure points.

options.label: B
options.text: Expand tool descriptions with input formats, example queries, and boundary conditions (e.g., 'process_refund: [reason, amount, refund_window_days]').
options.explanation: BEST: Clear, structured tool specs enable the agent to make correct routing decisions via its reasoning. Fixes root cause without overhead. CODE (explicit schema) > PROMPTS (vague descriptions).

options.label: C
options.text: Add few-shot examples of correct refund request handling in the system prompt.
options.explanation: Valid but suboptimal: Adds tokens and context pressure. Scales poorly as tool catalog grows. Explicit schema scales better than implicit examples.

options.label: D
options.text: Merge both tools into a single 'handle_order_action' tool that routes internally.
options.explanation: Valid but heavy-handed: Consolidation reduces agent autonomy and obscures intent. Overkill when the real issue is tool clarity.

questions.algorithmTrace: Root cause: ambiguous tool descriptions → agent confusion. Solution: explicit schema + boundary info (EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT, CODE > PROMPTS). Anti-pattern: NLP classifier = second language model = duplicate work.

questions.question: A multi-agent research system coordinates three agents: web_researcher, data_analyzer, report_writer. The system passes results via a shared 'findings' dict in session context. Logs show 12% of analysis contains outdated findings (agent B reads before agent A completes write). What architecture eliminates this race condition while preserving parallelism?

options.label: A
options.text: Add explicit sleep(2) delay in agent B before reading findings to wait for agent A completion.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Delay-based synchronization is brittle. 2 seconds may be insufficient for large datasets; adds unnecessary latency. Race conditions remain—only probabilistically hidden.

options.label: B
options.text: Use context:fork for each agent execution so A and B operate in isolated context namespaces; coordinator waits for A.result, then passes explicitly to B.input.
options.explanation: BEST: Forked contexts eliminate shared mutable state. Deterministic sequencing (A completes → result returned → B invoked) prevents races. ISOLATED > SHARED, EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: C
options.text: Add schema validation and retry logic: if B's findings don't match expected structure, re-invoke the read operation.
options.explanation: Valid but doesn't fix root cause: Validation catches symptoms (malformed data) but not races. Retries are probabilistic; same race may happen again.

options.label: D
options.text: Replace shared session context with a PostgreSQL database; agents read/write with transactions to guarantee consistency.
options.explanation: Plausible but over-engineered: Adds external infrastructure + latency. For intra-session coordination, forked contexts are the built-in solution. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM, COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

questions.algorithmTrace: Symptom: stale findings. Root: shared mutable state + concurrent access. Solution: forked contexts + explicit passing (deterministic ordering). Anti-patterns: delays (unreliable), validation (treats symptom), external DB (unnecessary infrastructure).

questions.question: A code generation agent struggles with complex requests: 'Generate a REST API with auth, validation, error handling, and structured logging.' Traces show it consistently skips validation and logging, producing incomplete skeleton code. Root cause: complex request exceeds effective agent focus capacity. How should task decomposition improve completion rates?

options.label: A
options.text: Use a single 'generate_complete_api' tool; add detailed system prompt examples of fully-layered APIs so the agent understands all requirements.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Examples help but don't enforce. Under token/attention pressure, agent still shortcuts. Prompt guidance < tool structure for multi-layer requirements.

options.label: B
options.text: Decompose into sequential tools: generate_base_api → add_authentication → add_validation → add_logging. Each tool has single responsibility; coordinator chains results.
options.explanation: BEST: Sequential tools force completion of each layer in order. Single-responsibility per tool prevents cognitive overload. EXPLICIT (ordered tasks) > IMPLICIT (self-directed). Coordinator ensures causality.

options.label: C
options.text: Increase max_tokens to 8000 and ask the agent to generate all layers in one call with explicit numbered sections.
options.explanation: Valid but risky: Larger token budget helps, but single call still allows corner-cutting under attention load. Numbered sections don't enforce completion. Tool structure > token increase alone.

options.label: D
options.text: Use fan-out parallelism: invoke 4 sub-agents (auth_specialist, validation_specialist, logging_specialist) in parallel, then merge results with a compositor.
options.explanation: Plausible but creates merging complexity: Parallel generation of auth + validation + logging may produce conflicting patterns or duplicate code. Merging 4 outputs is error-prone.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: multi-layer generation incomplete. Cause: complex request, single tool, cognitive overload. Fix: sequential tools (ordered, single responsibility). Anti-patterns: examples only (don't enforce), larger token budget (doesn't prevent shortcuts), parallel specialists (merging complexity).

questions.question: A support agent achieves 54% first-contact resolution vs. a 75% SLA target. Logs show it over-escalates simple password resets (80% solvable in-agent) but under-escalates complex billing disputes (only 20% of attempts succeed). Current instruction: 'Escalate if uncertain.' What is the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Train the agent to compute self-reported confidence after each response; escalate if confidence < 0.6.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated. Agent's confidence in its output ≠ its capability to resolve the issue. Overconfident on complex tasks, under-confident on simple ones.

options.label: B
options.text: Define explicit escalation rules as deterministic checks: 'if category==password_reset, do NOT escalate; if category==billing_dispute AND retry_count>2, escalate.' Include examples.
options.explanation: BEST: Explicit rules based on task category + retry count, not opinion. Data-driven thresholds (category, retry count) prevent both over/under-escalation. DETERMINISTIC > PROBABILISTIC for business rules.

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy a separate binary classifier (resolvable / non-resolvable) trained on historical support tickets to predict escalation need.
options.explanation: Valid but adds infrastructure: Requires training data, model maintenance, inference overhead. Explicit rules scale with domain knowledge; classifiers require data + retraining.

options.label: D
options.text: Analyze customer tone (sentiment) in messages; escalate if tone is very negative, as frustrated customers need human touch.
options.explanation: Plausible but wrong signal: Customer frustration ≠ task difficulty. An angry customer with a solvable password reset still should NOT be escalated; worse, escalation may increase frustration.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: both over- and under-escalation damage FCR. Root: 'uncertain' is vague, doesn't correlate with task resolvability. Fix: explicit category-based rules + retry thresholds (deterministic + data-driven). Anti-patterns: self-confidence (poorly calibrated), classifiers (infrastructure), sentiment (wrong signal).

questions.question: A CI/CD agent running integration tests faces flaky failures: 12% of test suite runs fail due to connection timeouts to staging database, but the same test passes on retry. Currently, the agent logs the failure and stops. Over 5000 monthly test runs, ~600 flaky failures block deployments. What error recovery strategy reduces flaky-failure impact?

options.label: A
options.text: Add exponential backoff retry loop: 3 retries with 5s, 10s, 20s delays. Log all failures, but consider the test 'passed' if ANY retry succeeds.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Silently masking flakiness ('passed if any retry succeeds') hides systemic issues. Real failures + flaky failures both fail, but with different root causes. Obscures which.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement structured error propagation: retry transient errors (timeout, connection_refused) up to 3x, but categorize and fail fast on permanent errors (assertion failure, auth error). Log error type for diagnosis.
options.explanation: BEST: Distinguishes transient (retry-worthy) vs. permanent (fail-fast). Retries only when beneficial. Structured logging (error category) enables root-cause analysis. EXPLICIT error classification > blanket retry.

options.label: C
options.text: Detect flaky tests by running the entire suite 3 times in parallel; consider a test 'passed' only if all 3 runs succeed.
options.explanation: Valid but expensive: 3x test execution + latency overhead. Serial retry (fix + diagnosis) is cheaper. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a circuit breaker: if timeout errors exceed 15% in a 10-min window, pause test suite and alert on-call engineer to restart the database.
options.explanation: Plausible but defers to humans: Useful for persistent outages, but transient flakiness (single-test timeout) doesn't warrant human intervention. Overcomplicates error handling.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: 12% flaky failures block deploys. Root: transient network timeouts. Solution: structured error classification (transient → retry, permanent → fail fast) + logging. Prevents silent masking. Anti-patterns: blanket retry (masks issues), parallel runs (cost), circuit breaker (for persistent outages, not transients).

questions.question: A support agent maintains state across a long customer conversation: conversation_id, customer_tier, open_order_ids, refund_eligibility_status. After 28 minutes, the agent's database connection drops, recovering after 90 seconds. The agent continues but loses state. Next customer response produces malformed output due to missing context. What design minimizes state loss impact?

options.label: A
options.text: Store all state in a persistent Redis cache with TTL; before each tool call, check if state expired or corrupted.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Pushing state to external cache increases latency and failure surface. Cache misses still cause data loss. Better: stateless agent + state passed in request.

options.label: B
options.text: Design agent as stateless: each turn receives full conversation history + derived state (tier, order_ids, eligibility) in the request; agent outputs response + updated state back to caller.
options.explanation: BEST: No agent-side persistence → no connection loss impact. Caller (conversation manager) handles state durability + recovery. Stateless agents are resilient and replaceable. CODE (explicit state passing) > state hiding.

options.label: C
options.text: Checkpoint the agent's state to disk every 30 seconds; on recovery, reload the checkpoint.
options.explanation: Valid but adds complexity: Disk I/O overhead, 30-second checkpoint lag still allows state loss. Stateless design (state in request) avoids this.

options.label: D
options.text: Use in-memory state + make agent restarts trigger a full state rebuild from database; block user input during rebuild.
options.explanation: Plausible but poor UX: Rebuild latency + forced user wait. Stateless agent handles this transparently without blocking.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: state loss on agent restart. Root: agent holds state in-process, restart wipes it. Solution: stateless design (state in request, caller manages durability). Anti-patterns: external cache (latency, still lossy), disk checkpoint (lag), in-memory + rebuild (latency).

questions.question: A multi-agent developer productivity system has: code_reviewer, test_generator, documentation_agent. They exchange intermediate results via a shared SQLite database 'shared_state.db' with a 'results' table. After 3 transactions, the database becomes locked (one agent holds a write lock), blocking the other two for 8-12 seconds per operation. What communication pattern eliminates lock contention?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase SQLite cache size and add WAL mode (write-ahead logging) for concurrent writes.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: WAL helps but SQLite has inherent lock limitations. For 3+ concurrent agents, better to change communication model entirely. Better tools exist for agent-to-agent messaging.

options.label: B
options.text: Replace database with a message queue (e.g., task queue or pub/sub): code_reviewer → review_complete event → test_generator subscribes, pulls findings, generates tests → test_complete event → doc_agent subscribes.
options.explanation: BEST: Pub/sub decouples agents; no lock contention. Each agent publishes results, others consume asynchronously. Scales with number of agents. MESSAGE PASSING > SHARED STATE.

options.label: C
options.text: Serialize agent execution: run code_reviewer → on completion, run test_generator → on completion, run doc_agent. Eliminate parallelism to avoid lock conflicts.
options.explanation: Valid but slow: Kills parallelism, increases wall-clock time. Tradeoff (concurrency for correctness) not worth it.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy separate database instance per agent, then use triggers to replicate writes across instances.
options.explanation: Plausible but complex: Replication lag + consistency issues. Message queue is simpler and more standard for agent communication.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: lock contention from 3 agents reading/writing shared database. Root: tight coupling via shared mutable state. Solution: pub/sub message passing (decoupled, event-driven). Anti-patterns: database tuning (doesn't fix model), serialization (no parallelism), replication (complexity).

questions.question: A data extraction system has one agent that both: (a) fetches raw documents from S3, (b) parses PDFs into structured data, (c) validates against schema, and (d) writes results to database. The agent is reused in two pipelines: one needs S3→parse, another needs parse→validate→write. Code sharing is tangled; changes to validation break the S3 pipeline. What architecture improves separation?

options.label: A
options.text: Add feature flags ('SKIP_S3', 'SKIP_DB_WRITE') to the agent so different pipelines can enable/disable steps.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Feature flags hide coupling; multiply conditions make code harder to test/maintain. Better: split into independent agents.

options.label: B
options.text: Decompose into four single-responsibility agents: s3_fetcher, pdf_parser, schema_validator, db_writer. Pipelines instantiate only needed agents in order.
options.explanation: BEST: Each agent does one thing. Reuse parse + validate in pipeline 2 without S3 fetch. SEPARATION OF CONCERNS > MONOLITHIC. Easy to test, version, and modify independently.

options.label: C
options.text: Use a middleware framework that wraps the monolithic agent and intercepts steps; skip steps at runtime based on pipeline config.
options.explanation: Valid but adds indirection: Middleware obfuscates the agent logic. Better to split at source.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a base agent class with the full pipeline; pipeline-specific subclasses override unwanted methods with 'pass'.
options.explanation: Plausible but brittle: Inheritance creates hidden dependencies. If base changes, subclasses break. Composition (separate agents) > inheritance.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: tangled agent logic reused across different pipelines; changes break consumers. Root: monolithic agent mixes concerns. Solution: decompose into single-responsibility agents (s3_fetch, parse, validate, write). Pipeline selects needed agents. Anti-patterns: feature flags (complex), middleware (indirect), inheritance (brittle).

questions.question: A developer productivity tool has two requirements: (1) generate code from requirements, (2) review generated code for bugs/style. Currently, a single agent tries to both: generates code, then runs self-review. But metrics show 28% of generated code fails review. Time-to-fix is 45 additional seconds per task. Cost per task is 1.8M tokens. What architecture reduces review failures?

options.label: A
options.text: Improve the system prompt with detailed style guide and bug-pattern examples; ask the agent to 'review carefully' before returning code.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Self-review is unreliable; agent repeats its own mistakes. Prompt improvements marginally help but don't fix the root (single agent can't objectify its own code).

options.label: B
options.text: Separate into two agents: generator (produces code) → reviewer (independent call, receives only code + requirements, no knowledge of generation). Fail if reviewer identifies issues; coordinator routes back to generator.
options.explanation: BEST: Separate agents = fresh perspective. Reviewer sees code as black box, doesn't repeat generator's biases. Explicit error loop enables retry. MULTI-AGENT > SELF-REVIEW for adversarial tasks.

options.label: C
options.text: Run the single agent 3 times in parallel, then use a voting/consensus mechanism to pick the best code.
options.explanation: Valid but expensive: 3x token cost + no review guarantee. 3 copies of same agent = 3 copies of same mistakes. Doesn't add fresh perspective.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy the code automatically; catch bugs in production tests and use that signal to retrain the agent.
options.explanation: Plausible but risky: Production bugs harm users. Catching bugs pre-deploy (via separate reviewer) is better. Post-deployment retraining is slow feedback.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: agent misses its own bugs (28% fail review). Root: self-review is unreliable; agent's generation bias persists in review. Solution: separate reviewer agent (fresh perspective, no generation bias). Explicit error loop → retry. Anti-patterns: prompt improvements (marginal), parallel voting (same agent), post-deploy detection (risky).

questions.question: A long-running data processing agent ingests 50,000 documents in a batch job lasting 8 hours. Checkpoint is saved only at the very end (success or complete failure). If the agent crashes after 6 hours (45,000 documents processed), the entire batch must restart from document 1. This wastes 6 hours of compute and delays delivery by 1 day. What checkpoint strategy reduces waste?

options.label: A
options.text: Checkpoint every document; store batch_id + processed_document_ids to a results database.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Checkpoint per-document = 50,000 writes, overhead exceeds benefit. Too granular; write I/O becomes bottleneck.

options.label: B
options.text: Checkpoint every 1000 documents: save agent state (processed_count, next_doc_id, derived_aggregates) with resumption marker. On restart, load checkpoint and continue from next_doc_id.
options.explanation: BEST: Coarse-grained checkpoints (every 1000 docs) balance I/O cost vs. recovery granularity. Allows restart from doc 45,001 (save 6 hours). DETERMINISTIC RECOVERY (explicit checkpoint) > hoping for no crashes.

options.label: C
options.text: Split batch into 10 sub-batches of 5000 docs each; run them in parallel. If one crashes, only 5000 docs re-process instead of 50,000.
options.explanation: Valid but different tradeoff: Parallelism adds infrastructure; sub-batch boundaries are artificial. Checkpointing within single batch is simpler.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy on a fault-tolerant cluster with automatic restart; agent state persists in shared memory across nodes.
options.explanation: Plausible but expensive: Infrastructure cost. Simple checkpointing solves this without cluster complexity. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: 6-hour crash = restart full 8-hour job. Root: no checkpoints. Solution: coarse-grained checkpoints (every 1000 docs) to balance I/O + recovery. Anti-patterns: per-doc checkpoints (overhead), parallel sub-batches (infrastructure), cluster restart (expensive).

questions.question: A customer support agent has access to 18 tools: order_lookup, order_cancel, refund_customer, update_customer_profile, delete_customer_record, export_customer_data_csv, disable_payment_method, access_credit_card_vault, suspend_account, escalate_to_manager, issue_coupon, etc. Logs show it occasionally calls delete_customer_record when it should escalate the request. What principle prevents mis-use?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep all 18 tools available; add detailed warnings in tool descriptions ('WARNING: DELETES CUSTOMER AND ALL DATA').
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Warnings in descriptions don't prevent misuse; agent may overlook or misinterpret. Better to reject dangerous calls at the tool boundary.

options.label: B
options.text: Restrict agent to 6 read-only / safe tools (order_lookup, customer_profile_view, escalate_to_manager); dangerous tools (delete, export, vault access) require explicit human approval per call.
options.explanation: BEST: Least privilege principle: agent has minimal necessary tools for common cases. Dangerous tools require human gate. EXPLICIT GATING > warnings. Prevents accidental misuse.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a pre-call validator that rejects any delete_ or export_ call and returns a message: 'This action requires manual review.'
options.explanation: Valid but incomplete: Works, but forces coordinator/human to implement this logic. Better: restrict tools at agent definition, not post-hoc.

options.label: D
options.text: Add confidence scoring to each tool call; block calls with low confidence (e.g., 'delete_customer' with confidence < 0.9).
options.explanation: Plausible but unreliable: Confidence thresholds are probabilistic and agent-calibration varies. Better: explicit tool restrictions (deterministic).

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: agent calls delete_customer accidentally. Root: overly broad tool permission set. Solution: least privilege (restrict to safe tools) + explicit gating (approval for dangerous ops). Anti-patterns: warnings alone (ignored), post-hoc validator (complexity), confidence scores (unreliable).

questions.question: A financial data extraction agent uses a 'table_parser' tool. Tool description: 'Parses tables.' After 500 invocations, logs show: agent calls table_parser on plain-text data (not a table), tool returns empty result, agent re-invokes it 3-5 times before giving up. 18% failure rate. Developer finds tool is designed ONLY for HTML tables with specific <table> tags. Current description masks this requirement. How should tool design improve clarity?

options.label: A
options.text: Add example invocations to system prompt: 'Here are 5 examples of table_parser on HTML tables...'
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: System prompt examples don't scale; max 5-10 examples, but failures come from edge cases outside those examples. Better: explicit schema.

options.label: B
options.text:
Rewrite tool description: 'Parses HTML elements with TR/TD structure. Input: HTML string. Output: rows (list of dicts). Returns empty if no
tag found. Example: table_parser(html="
A
") → [{"A": value}, ...]'
options.explanation: BEST: Explicit schema (input format, output structure, boundary conditions, example). Agent immediately understands when to call it and what to expect. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT, CODE > PROMPTS.

options.label: C
options.text: Rename tool from 'table_parser' to 'html_table_parser' so the name hints at HTML requirement.
options.explanation: Valid but insufficient: Name hints help but don't guarantee understanding. Agent may still try it on non-HTML. Schema is authoritative.

options.label: D
options.text: Add validation inside the tool: if input is not HTML, return error message 'Input must be HTML <table> element.' Agent learns from errors on retry.
options.explanation: Plausible but reactive: Tool rejects invalid input, but agent doesn't know why until calling it (18% failures). Better: proactive clarity (explicit schema).

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: 18% failures from agent misusing table_parser on non-HTML. Root: vague tool description ('Parses tables'). Solution: explicit schema + boundary conditions + example. Agent avoids invalid calls upfront. Anti-patterns: system prompt examples (limited scale), name hints (indirect), post-hoc validation (reactive).

questions.question: A multi-agent research system (web_searcher, analyst) feeds a report_writer agent. Web_searcher encounters rate limit (429 HTTP status) after fetching 4 of 12 required sources. Current behavior: agent crashes, entire report generation fails. Goal: generate best-effort report with available data. What architecture enables graceful degradation?

options.label: A
options.text: Catch the 429 error and retry indefinitely (exponential backoff) until the server responds.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Ignores the constraint. Indefinite retry blocks the pipeline and wastes time. Rate limit is a signal to stop and adapt.

options.label: B
options.text: Return {status: 'partial_failure', data: [4 sources fetched], missing: [8 sources required]}; analyst processes available data with confidence penalty; report_writer generates report marked 'Based on 33% available data'.
options.explanation: BEST: Structured error propagation (status + partial data). Downstream agents adapt (analyst adjusts confidence, writer adds disclaimer). EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT. CODE (structured error) > PROMPTS (hope agent infers from crash).

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a fallback: if web_searcher fails, use cached results from previous run or public summary APIs.
options.explanation: Valid but adds infrastructure: Caching + fallback APIs complicate deployment. Better: accept and propagate what you have.

options.label: D
options.text: Increase timeout and retry budget; if still rate-limited, escalate to human analyst to manually fetch sources.
options.explanation: Plausible but unscalable: Human intervention for every rate limit defeats automation. Better: graceful degradation (partial data → reduced-confidence report).

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: rate limit stops entire pipeline. Root: no graceful degradation strategy. Solution: return partial data + status; downstream agents adapt (analyst: lower confidence, writer: add disclaimer). Anti-patterns: infinite retry (ignores constraint), caching (infrastructure), human escalation (unscalable).

questions.question: A structured data extraction agent processes expense report images: extracts vendor name, amount, category, receipt date. 85% are routine (amount < $500, vendor is standard). 15% are edge cases (amount > $2000, vendor unknown, or date smudged). Currently, ALL results go to a human approver (average 4 min per report, $0.02 cost per review). Company spends $40K/month on approval labor. What approval strategy reduces cost?

options.label: A
options.text: Remove human review entirely; trust agent 100%. Save $40K/month in review cost.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: No guardrails. Errors accumulate; compliance and fraud risk increase. False economy.

options.label: B
options.text: Define confidence threshold: auto-approve extractions where agent confidence > 0.95 (routine), send only <0.95 to human. Routes 85% auto, 15% to human.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Agent self-confidence is poorly calibrated and independent of actual correctness. High-confidence errors still slip through.

options.label: C
options.text: Use explicit rules: auto-approve if (amount < $500 AND vendor in {standard_list} AND date clear); send to human only if any condition fails.
options.explanation: BEST: Data-driven rules (deterministic, not probabilistic). Route 85% routine → auto-approve. 15% edge cases → human review (reduces labor to $6K/month). DETERMINISTIC > CONFIDENCE. LEAST PRIVILEGE for approval.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy two independent extraction agents; if both agree, auto-approve; if they differ, send to human.
options.explanation: Plausible but expensive: 2 agents = 2x inference cost. Consensus doesn't improve accuracy (same agent patterns repeated). Better: explicit rules.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: $40K/month approval cost; 85% are routine. Root: uniform approval for all results. Solution: explicit rules (auto-approve routine, human gates edge cases). Reduces approval to 15% (cost → ~$6K). Anti-patterns: no review (risk), confidence thresholds (poorly calibrated), dual agents (cost).

questions.question: An agent implementing ReAct pattern (Think → Act → Observe → Loop) is extracting claim amounts from emails with complex multi-paragraph context. On a complex email, the agent enters an infinite loop: 'Observe → Hmm, I need another tool' (20+ rounds, 400K tokens). No natural stopping condition; max_iterations=50 is hit. Goal: ensure agent halts after solving the problem. What is the most effective stopping condition?

options.label: A
options.text: Lower max_iterations to 5; agent must adapt and stop early.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Arbitrary limit creates premature exits on complex-but-solvable queries. Better: condition based on task state, not iteration count.

options.label: B
options.text: Implement an explicit stopping rule: 'If (task_objective == SOLVED) OR (no_new_information_in_last_3_turns), return answer.' Add tool 'mark_task_complete' for agent to invoke when done.
options.explanation: BEST: Task state (objective reached) + stagnation detection (no progress in 3 turns) → explicit halt. Agent controls exit via tool. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT, CODE > max_iterations count.

options.label: C
options.text: Measure perplexity of agent's reasoning across iterations; if perplexity increases (agent confused), stop and escalate.
options.explanation: Valid but indirect: Perplexity is hard to measure reliably. Explicit state (task complete vs. task unsolved) is clearer.

options.label: D
options.text: Set a token budget (e.g., 50K tokens); halt once budget exceeded.
options.explanation: Plausible but blunt: Token limits are cost-driven, not task-driven. Complex legitimate queries may exceed budget; simple ones may not use it. Task completion is better signal.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: infinite loop in ReAct → hits max_iterations (premature halts or wastes tokens). Root: no explicit completion condition. Solution: task state (objective reached?) + stagnation (no progress in N turns?) → halt. Anti-patterns: low iteration limit (premature), perplexity (hard to measure), token budget (costs-driven, not task-driven).

questions.question: A structured extraction agent processes invoices: (required fields: vendor_name, invoice_id, amount, date; optional fields: tax, discount, line_items). Logs show 22% of extracted records are missing required_fields or have extraction confidence < 0.7. These records are sent downstream to accounting, creating reject-and-rescan cycles. How should extraction architecture handle coverage gaps?

options.label: A
options.text: Ask agent to re-scan if missing fields: prompt 'If any required field is empty, please re-extract more carefully.'
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: Prompt-based self-correction is unreliable; agent repeats extraction mistakes. Doesn't add validation logic.

options.label: B
options.text: After extraction, validate schema: check all required_fields present + confidence > 0.7. If validation fails, return status='incomplete' + missing_fields list. Route to human for manual extraction.
options.explanation: BEST: Structured validation (schema check + threshold) is deterministic and explicit. Prevents broken records downstream. CODE (validation hook) > PROMPTS. Explicit > implicit coverage requirements.

options.label: C
options.text: Use lower confidence threshold (0.5 instead of 0.7) and auto-approve low-confidence entries if all required fields present.
options.explanation: Valid but incorrect: Lower threshold increases false positives. Confidence < 0.7 means field is unreliable, regardless of presence. Conflates presence with reliability.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy a second 'verifier' agent to review extracted records and fix low-confidence/missing fields.
options.explanation: Plausible but expensive: Second agent = extra cost + latency. Deterministic validation (hook) is simpler and faster.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: 22% incomplete extractions sent downstream → rejections. Root: no pre-downstream validation. Solution: schema validation (required fields present + confidence > threshold) + explicit routing (incomplete → human). Anti-patterns: prompt self-correction (unreliable), lower threshold (masks unreliability), second agent (expensive).

questions.question: Your support team's agent has access to 8 tools: `get_customer`, `lookup_order`, `check_inventory`, `apply_discount`, `process_refund`, `escalate_case`, `send_email`, and `update_ticket_status`. Production metrics show that the agent correctly selects the right tool only 71% of the time. Agents frequently call `apply_discount` when users ask about order status, and `process_refund` when items are out of stock. What's the most significant architectural problem?

options.label: A
options.text: Tool descriptions are insufficiently detailed to disambiguate purposes. Consolidate to 4-5 core tools with comprehensive descriptions that explicitly state when each tool should and should not be used
options.explanation: The >5 tools anti-pattern degrades selection accuracy because the model struggles to differentiate between similar tools without explicit boundaries. 8 tools exceeds the cognitive load threshold. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM: improve descriptions and reduce tool count before adding prompt guidance. Accuracy should improve to 85%+.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a routing classifier that parses the user request and pre-selects the most likely tool before passing it to the agent
options.explanation: A pre-routing classifier bypasses the agent's natural language understanding and introduces coupling. Tool selection should remain probabilistic via descriptions, not deterministic via classification. NL parsing for control flow is an anti-pattern.

options.label: C
options.text: Add 10-15 few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection for each tool type
options.explanation: Few-shot examples can improve consistency from 71% to ~75%, but they don't fix the root cause: too many tools with weak descriptions. With 8 tools, examples add token overhead without proportional gain.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement a tool logging layer that tracks agent mistakes and automatically blocks misused tools in future calls
options.explanation: Tool blocking is reactive, not architectural. It doesn't improve the agent's ability to select correctly from remaining tools. Doesn't address the root cause of weak descriptions or tool count.

questions.algorithmTrace: B & D are reactive/architectural workarounds. C adds overhead without fixing root cause: 8 tools exceed anti-pattern threshold (>5). A directly addresses tool count AND description quality → BUILT-IN > CUSTOM → select A.

questions.question: You're building an MCP server for a code analysis tool. Your tool accepts a `file_path` parameter that can be a relative path like `src/utils/helpers.ts`, an absolute path like `/home/user/project/src/utils.ts`, or a glob pattern like `src/**/*.test.ts`. Currently, the agent frequently passes incorrect path formats, causing 34% of calls to fail with validation errors. What schema design change would most reliably improve success rate?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep `file_path` as a free-text string but add extensive examples to the tool description showing all supported formats and error messages for invalid formats
options.explanation: Free-text strings rely on the model remembering format rules from descriptions. With 3 different valid formats, this remains probabilistic. Examples help but don't prevent mistakes.

options.label: B
options.text: Replace the single `file_path` parameter with three separate enum parameters: `path_type` (enum: relative|absolute|glob) and `path_value` (string)
options.explanation: Splitting into separate parameters with enums helps slightly, but requires the agent to reason about which type to use. Still requires correct reasoning about path formats.

options.label: C
options.text: Replace `file_path` with a single `path_format` enum (relative | absolute | glob) and let the agent specify the format explicitly, then accept the path as a second required parameter
options.explanation: Atomic parameters with clear enums force the agent to explicitly specify the format, eliminating ambiguity. The enum constrains choices, reducing the decision space. Success rate should jump to 96%+. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement client-side validation in your MCP client that auto-detects path format and normalizes it before sending to the server
options.explanation: Client-side validation is a workaround that hides the real problem: the agent doesn't understand path formats. It doesn't improve the agent's reliability, just masks failures.

questions.algorithmTrace: A = free-text → probabilistic → weaker. B = still requires reasoning about type. D = client-side workaround. C = enum + atomic parameters → EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT → eliminates ambiguity → select C.

questions.question: Your development team uses an MCP server for analyzing code quality metrics. Currently, the server runs as a local stdio process on each developer's machine, with 50+ developers connecting simultaneously. During peak usage, response times spike from 200ms to 4.2 seconds, and 8% of requests timeout. Your infrastructure team reports the bottleneck is the sqlite3 database on the shared network drive becoming contended. What transport architecture change would you prioritize?

options.label: A
options.text: Migrate to a centralized SSE-based MCP server solution with load balancing across 3 instances, sharing a cloud PostgreSQL database
options.explanation: SSE (Server-Sent Events) enables a centralized architecture where the database bottleneck moves from client-side sqlite3 to a properly scaled PostgreSQL cluster with connection pooling. Load balancing distributes requests. Response times normalize to <300ms. This is the appropriate architectural scale for 50+ concurrent users.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the stdio-based architecture but move the shared sqlite3 database to a faster SSD storage tier and add read replicas
options.explanation: Optimizing sqlite3 provides incremental improvement (maybe 3.2s response time) but doesn't address the fundamental problem: sqlite3 is not designed for 50+ concurrent writers. Contention remains.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement caching in each local stdio process using an in-memory LRU cache to reduce database queries by 70%
options.explanation: Caching helps for repeated queries but doesn't address cache coherency across 50 instances. Developers would see inconsistent data. Also doesn't solve the 8% timeout issue (slow writes).

options.label: D
options.text: Reduce the number of concurrent developers to 15 per MCP server instance and scale horizontally with separate server instances per team
options.explanation: Artificial capacity limits don't scale and adds operational overhead. The real problem is the transport architecture isn't suitable for the scale. SSE with proper centralization is the right fix.

questions.algorithmTrace: B & C are optimizations, not architectural fixes. D is artificial limiting. A migrates from stdio (local scale) to SSE (distributed scale) with proper database → addresses root cause of contention → select A.

questions.question: You're designing an MCP server that provides research resources across 3 domains: medicine, finance, and law. The server has 150,000 research papers across these domains. Your design has two options: (1) Create a single `search_research` tool that accepts a domain parameter and searches all 150K papers; (2) Create three separate tools (`search_medical_research`, `search_financial_research`, `search_legal_research`) each with 50K papers pre-indexed. Option 2 uses MCP resources to provide domain-specific resource templates. Which design principle favors option 2?

options.label: A
options.text: Option 2 creates unnecessary tool duplication and violates DRY principles. A single `search_research` tool with domain filtering is more maintainable
options.explanation: While DRY has merit, the CCA-F principles prioritize reliability over code conciseness. Separate tools allow separate index optimization and reduce the search space, improving response quality. Tool duplication is sometimes the right choice when it improves UX.

options.label: B
options.text: Option 2 allows the agent to reason about domain boundaries explicitly via tool selection, reducing hallucination of cross-domain legal-medical correlations that the single tool might accidentally generate
options.explanation: Separate tools with MCP resource templates enforce domain separation at the architectural level. The agent's tool selection becomes a signal about domain relevance BEFORE search executes. Reduces spurious correlations. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT: separate tools make domain intent explicit.

options.label: C
options.text: Option 2 requires more API endpoints, increasing implementation complexity. Option 1 is simpler to build and maintain
options.explanation: Implementation complexity is a weaker factor than reliability. The question asks which design principle favors option 2 — the principle is domain separation and selection reliability, not code simplicity.

options.label: D
options.text: Option 2 requires three separate MCP resource definitions with separate authentication, which is more overhead than a single tool with role-based access
options.explanation: This confuses resources (data templates) with tool definitions (API functions). Resources and authentication are orthogonal. Option 2's benefit isn't about authentication — it's about tool selection making domain intent explicit.

questions.algorithmTrace: A = prioritizes code metrics over reliability. C = prioritizes implementation simplicity. D = confuses resources with auth. B directly states: separate tools → agent selects domain explicitly → reduces hallucination → EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT → select B.

questions.question: Your extraction tool processes 50,000 documents monthly and returns structured data to the agent. Currently, the tool returns unstructured text results like 'Extraction succeeded with 12 fields found, confidence 0.87' or 'ERROR: PDF parsing failed on page 3'. Downstream tools frequently misinterpret these results, leading to 13% of extractions being silently dropped or incorrect. What's the most effective solution?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a post-processing step in the agent that parses the text results with regex to extract confidence scores and error types before passing to downstream tools
options.explanation: Regex-based post-processing is fragile — it must be updated whenever the text format changes. Also adds latency and doesn't solve the root cause: the tool isn't returning structured results.

options.label: B
options.text: Return structured JSON from the tool with required fields: `{success: boolean, extracted_fields: {...}, confidence: number, error_code?: string, error_message?: string}`
options.explanation: Structured JSON results eliminate parsing ambiguity. Downstream tools can reliably branch on `success` and access `confidence` numerically. Error codes allow programmatic handling. This is LLM consumption best practice: explicit > implicit structure.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement strict error handling where failed extractions throw exceptions rather than returning error messages, forcing the agent to catch and handle specific exception types
options.explanation: Throwing exceptions is worse for LLM tool consumption than returning structured errors. Exceptions aren't part of the tool's contract and can cause unpredictable agent behavior.

options.label: D
options.text: Add verbose logging inside the tool so the agent can examine detailed logs to understand extraction results
options.explanation: Logs are for debugging, not for tool result consumption. The agent can't reliably parse logs for decision-making. Results must be in the tool's response body as structured data.

questions.algorithmTrace: A = fragile regex parsing → anti-pattern. C = exceptions bad for LLM tools. D = logs aren't tool results. B = structured JSON → explicit, reliable consumption → select B.

questions.question: Your CI/CD orchestration tool exposes a `run_build_step` tool that executes shell commands. When a build step fails, the tool currently returns: `'Build failed: exit code 1'`. Agents frequently escalate as 'fatal errors' and abort the pipeline, even when the failure is a known, recoverable issue (e.g., 'npm install flakiness' that passes on retry). The failure detection is 100% accurate in logs, but the tool doesn't convey context. What error handling design would most reduce false escalations?

options.label: A
options.text: Return structured error responses with: `{success: boolean, exit_code: number, stderr?: string, error_category: enum(FLAKY | TIMEOUT | MISSING_DEPENDENCY | SYNTAX_ERROR | ...), is_retryable: boolean}`
options.explanation: Structured errors with semantic categorization (`error_category` enum) and retry guidance (`is_retryable` boolean) allow the agent to make intelligent decisions: flaky errors → retry, syntax errors → escalate. This prevents false escalations. Explicit error structure >>> implicit text messages.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a heuristic to the agent's system prompt: 'If a build fails due to npm flakiness, automatically retry up to 3 times before escalating'
options.explanation: Hard-coded retry heuristics in prompts are brittle and don't scale. They also require prompt updates when failure modes change. The tool should communicate the retry decision, not the agent.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement automatic retry logic inside `run_build_step` so the tool itself retries flaky commands transparently, returning only the final result
options.explanation: Tool-side retries can mask real issues and consume quota. The agent needs visibility into attempt history to make decisions. Transparency is better than hidden retries.

options.label: D
options.text: Parse the stderr output from the failed command using regex patterns to detect keywords like 'ENOTFOUND' and 'timeout', then add those keywords to the tool's error message
options.explanation: Regex-based keyword detection is fragile and doesn't scale across languages/frameworks. Semantic categories (enum) are more reliable.

questions.algorithmTrace: B = prompt heuristics → fragile. C = hides retry context. D = regex keywords → fragile. A = structured errors with semantic categories and retry guidance → enables intelligent agent decisions → select A.

questions.question: Your agent has two tools: `get_customer` (returns customer metadata) and `get_customer_orders` (returns customer's order history). Both require a `customer_id`. The API rate limits show `get_customer` allows 1,000 req/min while `get_customer_orders` allows 100 req/min. Merging them into a single `get_customer_full` tool that returns both customer data AND order history in one call would be simpler. However, many workflows only need customer metadata (e.g., for account verification), and combining them would unnecessarily consume order-history rate limit quota. What architectural principle favors keeping them separate?

options.label: A
options.text: Separation of concerns — combining different data types into one tool violates SRP (Single Responsibility Principle)
options.explanation: While SRP is good design practice, it's not the primary CCA-F principle here. The real driver is the rate limit constraints, not code organization.

options.label: B
options.text: Least privilege — keeping tools separate respects rate limit constraints and allows the agent to request only the data needed for each workflow, avoiding unnecessary quota consumption
options.explanation: LEAST PRIVILEGE is a CCA-F principle: tools should expose the minimum data/capability needed for their intended use. Combining them wastes quota when only one is needed. Separate tools allow fine-grained access control.

options.label: C
options.text: Consistency — if any two tools can be merged, they should be combined into larger tools to reduce API surface area
options.explanation: This inverts the principle. Reducing API surface area isn't a goal when it increases quota consumption and complexity.

options.label: D
options.text: Flexibility — keeping them separate makes it easier to replace the order-fetching implementation later without affecting customer metadata lookups
options.explanation: Implementation flexibility is a weaker consideration than quota constraints. The question specifically calls out rate limits as a concern.

questions.algorithmTrace: A = invokes SRP but not primary. C = contradicts principle. D = flexibility is weaker. B directly invokes LEAST PRIVILEGE + respects rate limit constraints → select B.

questions.question: Your code generation MCP server currently defines a single `generate_code` tool with a `language` parameter that accepts free-text values (e.g., 'TypeScript', 'Python', 'Go', 'Ruby', 'C#', 'Rust', 'Java'). Agents frequently generate code in unintended languages or misspell language names ('typescript' vs 'TypeScript', 'node' vs. 'JavaScript'). You're considering two schema redesigns: (A) Keep the `language` parameter as free-text but add strict validation and add detailed examples to the tool description; (B) Change `language` to an enum with exactly 7 allowed values. Which approach aligns with CCA-F tool design principles?

options.label: A
options.text: Enum constraints are overkill — tool descriptions should be sufficient to guide the agent. Free-text with examples is more flexible and accommodates future languages
options.explanation: Free-text + examples remain probabilistic and don't prevent errors. 'Flexible' for future languages is the wrong tradeoff — it sacrifices immediate reliability for theoretical future extensibility. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: B
options.text: Enum constraint on `language` is the correct schema design because it eliminates ambiguity and forces the agent to choose from valid options, preventing misspellings and out-of-scope language requests
options.explanation: Enums are explicit constraints that reduce the decision space. No misspellings possible, no agent confusion about valid values. Errors drop from 7% to <1%. Adding new languages requires schema update, but that's acceptable. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: C
options.text: Use a routing classifier that detects the language from context clues in the prompt, bypassing the parameter entirely
options.explanation: NL parsing for control flow is an anti-pattern. The parameter should remain explicit, not inferred.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep free-text but add runtime validation that rejects invalid languages and forces the agent to retry with a valid language from a list
options.explanation: Runtime validation (error + retry loop) wastes tokens and latency compared to schema-level enums that prevent the invalid call upfront.

questions.algorithmTrace: A = prioritizes false flexibility. C = NL inference anti-pattern. D = runtime validation > inefficient. B = enum constraint at schema level → EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT, deterministic selection → select B.

questions.question: Your MCP server provides a `query_code_repository` tool that accepts a commit SHA as input. Currently, the commit SHA parameter accepts any string (e.g., '3a7f9b2', 'main', 'HEAD~5', 'refs/tags/v1.2.0'). In 18% of agent calls, the tool receives an ambiguous or outdated reference (e.g., 'main' when the agent needed the previous main from 3 days ago), leading to incorrect code analysis. You want to support backward compatibility with existing clients. What versioning approach would allow stricter validation without breaking existing clients?

options.label: A
options.text: Release a new `v2` of the tool with stricter validation requiring full 40-character SHAs, deprecate the old `query_code_repository_v1` that accepts loose references, provide a migration guide
options.explanation: Tool versioning allows backward compatibility while enforcing strictness in new versions. Keep `v1` accepting flexible refs, release `v2` requiring full SHAs. Clients opt-in to stricter version. Deprecation signals intent without breaking existing usage.

options.label: B
options.text: Add optional `strict_mode: boolean` parameter to the existing tool; when true, it rejects loose references
options.explanation: Optional parameters add complexity but don't enforce strictness — agents can still forget to set `strict_mode: true`. It's opt-in, placing burden on the agent rather than tool design.

options.label: C
options.text: Immediately change the schema to only accept 40-char SHAs; add a conversion utility the agent can use to convert references to SHAs first
options.explanation: Breaking change without versioning violates backward compatibility. Forcing agents to pre-compute SHAs adds complexity and doesn't eliminate agent confusion.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the current loose parameter but mask ambiguities serverside by automatically resolving loose references to the most recent commit
options.explanation: Masking the problem (resolving ambiguously) reduces reliability. The agent should be forced to specify intent — what if 'main' resolved to a different SHA than intended?

questions.algorithmTrace: B = optional params don't enforce. C = breaks compatibility. D = masks ambiguity. A = versioning with deprecation → maintains compatibility while enforcing strictness → EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT → select A.

questions.question: You're designing an MCP server that provides resources for academic research: papers, datasets, and methodology templates. Currently, you've implemented a simple `get_resource` tool that returns static files (papers stored in S3, datasets in a data lake, templates in Git). You're considering adding MCP resource types to expose these resources as templates so agents can browse available resources before requesting them. What advantage would resource types provide over a single `get_resource` tool?

options.label: A
options.text: Resource types allow agents to enumerate available resources without making individual lookup calls, reducing API calls and enabling smarter selection
options.explanation: MCP resources provide metadata/templates that let agents introspect what's available (e.g., list of 500 papers with abstracts) before requesting specific ones. This enables better tool selection and filtering, reducing wasted calls. Dynamic resource discovery >> static tool parameters.

options.label: B
options.text: Resource types are just a naming/organizational convenience with no functional difference from tool parameters
options.explanation: Resource types aren't just naming — they're a distinct MCP concept that enables server-side resource enumeration and template discovery, which tools alone don't provide.

options.label: C
options.text: Resource types eliminate the need for the `get_resource` tool entirely, since Claude can just read resources directly from the server
options.explanation: Resources and tools serve different purposes. Resources expose data/templates, tools expose actions. Both are needed: agents use resources to discover what's available, tools to fetch/process.

options.label: D
options.text: Resource types require the agent to construct complex resource URIs, making it harder to use than a simple `get_resource(name)` tool
options.explanation: This confuses MCP implementation details with usability. Resource discovery actually makes URI construction easier — agents can browse templates before referencing them.

questions.algorithmTrace: B = ignores functional value. C = confuses resources vs tools. D = inverts complexity. A = resource discovery enables smarter selection + reduces calls → BUILT-IN MCP feature → select A.

questions.question: Your extraction tool processes invoices and must extract 6 required fields: `vendor_name`, `invoice_id`, `invoice_date`, `total_amount`, `payment_terms`, and `line_items` (array of products/amounts). Currently, the tool returns results as plain text: 'Vendor: Acme Inc, Invoice ID: INV-2024-001, ...' This format is ambiguous when vendors have complex names or dates are formatted differently across regions. What's the correct tool result schema design that would enable reliable downstream processing?

options.label: A
options.text: Return structured JSON with typed fields: `{vendor_name: string, invoice_id: string, invoice_date: ISO8601, total_amount: {value: number, currency: string}, payment_terms: string, line_items: [{description: string, quantity: number, unit_price: number, total: number}, ...], extraction_confidence: number}`
options.explanation: Structured JSON with explicit types eliminates parsing ambiguity. Dates are ISO8601, amounts include currency, line_items is explicitly an array. Downstream tools can rely on structure: check `extraction_confidence > 0.9`, sum `line_items[].total` reliably. This is LLM + downstream tool consumption best practice.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the plain-text format but add a detailed extraction report as accompanying metadata that the agent can parse for validation
options.explanation: Metadata parsing is fragile. The core result should be structured so its reliability doesn't depend on the agent parsing a separate report.

options.label: C
options.text: Return results as HTML table for human readability, which systems can also parse for machine consumption
options.explanation: HTML isn't a reliable data format for machine consumption vs JSON. Parsing HTML is brittle.

options.label: D
options.text: Return separate responses for each field: first call returns vendor, second returns invoice_id, etc., forcing the agent to reconstruct the full invoice
options.explanation: Splitting into separate calls wastes latency and resources. All related fields should return atomically in one structured response.

questions.algorithmTrace: B = relies on agent parsing metadata. C = HTML unreliable. D = excessive calls. A = atomic, typed schema with explicit structure → enables reliable downstream processing → select A.

questions.question: Your team of 8 backend engineers maintains a monorepo with three services: authentication, payments, and inventory. Each service has different coding standards: the auth service requires async/await patterns with typed errors, the payments service uses callback-based error handling, and inventory uses try-catch blocks. You want Claude Code to automatically enforce each service's standards whenever developers create or edit code, without requiring manual intervention. The team is distributed across time zones and runs both IDE and CLI workflows. What is the most maintainable long-term approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Create .claude/rules/auth-patterns.yaml, .claude/rules/payments-patterns.yaml, and .claude/rules/inventory-patterns.yaml at each service's root, each with explicit glob patterns (e.g., services/auth/**/*.ts) and specific coding requirements
options.explanation: Project-scoped rules in .claude/rules/ automatically apply based on explicit glob patterns. Each rule file enforces service-specific standards deterministically. Developers never need to remember or invoke anything. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT. PROJECT SCOPE > PERSONAL SCOPE.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a single ~/.claude/rules/team-standards.yaml in the shared dotfiles that lists all three services' patterns and their glob path filters
options.explanation: Personal-scope rules (~/.claude/) apply to all projects, not service-specific ones. Would enforce inventory patterns in the auth service, conflicting with auth requirements. PROJECT SCOPE > PERSONAL SCOPE.

options.label: C
options.text: Create .claude/commands/enforce-patterns with a custom slash command that developers invoke to lint and fix code before committing
options.explanation: Commands require developer action — conflicts with 'without requiring manual intervention.' Work must be done before every commit, causing friction in the time-zone-distributed team.

options.label: D
options.text: Use a CLAUDE.md at the repo root with --auto-enforce flag to activate deterministic pattern matching across all subdirectories
options.explanation: The --auto-enforce flag does not exist in Claude Code. This is a fabricated feature attempting to mimic the correct solution's intent but with syntax that won't work.

questions.algorithmTrace: Requirement: automatic, no manual intervention. C eliminated (requires invoke). B wrong scope (personal applies to all projects, not service-specific). D fabricated feature. A uses project-scoped rules with explicit globs → deterministic, service-specific, automatic → select A.

questions.question: Your team generates database migration scripts using Claude Code. Currently, migrations are created at db/migrations/{timestamp}_description.sql. You want Claude Code to automatically execute these migrations against a development database immediately after generation, but only for files matching the migration directory pattern, and only when running in CI/CD pipelines (not in developer laptops). The same team also generates utility helper functions that should never auto-execute. How should you configure this?

options.label: A
options.text: Create .claude/hooks/PostToolUse.yaml that listens for file_create events on db/migrations/**/*.sql and executes a pre-defined database sync command
options.explanation: PostToolUse hooks trigger after Claude Code creates tools/files matching glob patterns. Combined with CI/CD environment checks, this enforces automatic migration execution on pipeline runs only. HOOKS > PROMPTS for deterministic workflows.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a .claude/settings.json configuration with autoExecuteMigrations: true and migrationPath: 'db/migrations/**/*.sql'
options.explanation: settings.json doesn't have an autoExecuteMigrations option. This is plausible-sounding but not an actual feature. Settings control tool access, not execution triggers.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a .claude/commands/migrate that developers manually invoke after file generation to run the sync
options.explanation: Requires developer action post-generation. Defeats the goal of 'immediately after generation.' Also would run on developer laptops if they invoke it.

options.label: D
options.text: Use PreToolUse hooks to intercept database operations and validate migration syntax before allowing execution
options.explanation: PreToolUse is for validation/blocking before tool use. Solves the wrong problem — you need post-generation execution, not pre-validation. PostToolUse > PreToolUse for triggering workflows.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need automatic execution after file generation, CI/CD only. C requires manual invoke (eliminates). B fabricated setting (eliminates). D solves validation, not execution (eliminates). A uses PostToolUse hooks with glob matching and environment awareness → correct → select A.

questions.question: You manage a Vite + React monorepo where engineers write component tests in three different files collocated with components: test.tsx, .test.ts, and spec.ts. Testing patterns also vary wildly: describe/it (Vitest), test() (Jest), and raw expect() assertions. A single .claude/rules/ file cannot enforce both file naming uniformity and internal test syntax simultaneously. You want Claude Code to enforce a consistent pattern whenever developers touch any test file. What's the best structural approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Create separate .claude/rules/test-naming.yaml (glob: **/*.test.tsx) and .claude/rules/test-syntax.yaml (glob: **/*.test.tsx) both targeting the unified naming convention, each with different constraints
options.explanation: Multiple rules files in .claude/rules/ all apply cumulatively. Each rule can focus on a specific aspect (naming vs syntax) with overlapping globs. Achieves comprehensive enforcement without a monolithic file. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a single heavyweight .claude/rules/tests.yaml that lists all naming patterns and all syntax patterns in one file with complex conditional logic
options.explanation: Single monolithic rules file becomes difficult to maintain and debug. Adding new test patterns requires editing a large file. Multiple focused rules are more maintainable.

options.label: C
options.text: Create .claude/commands/validate-tests that developers run before committing to check both naming and syntax compliance
options.explanation: Requires developer action before every commit. Doesn't prevent incorrect patterns from being written in the first place. Also inconsistent with time-zone-distributed teams relying on async work.

options.label: D
options.text: Use CLAUDE_TEST_ENFORCEMENT environment variable in the root CLAUDE.md to enable both naming and syntax validation across the repo
options.explanation: Environment variables are not a Claude Code feature for rule enforcement. This is a fabricated approach. Rules are configured via .claude/rules/ YAML files, not environment variables.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need to enforce two different aspects (naming + syntax) on same files. C requires manual invoke (eliminates). D fabricated feature (eliminates). B monolithic, hard to maintain (weak). A multiple rules files, each focused, cumulative application → correct → select A.

questions.question: Your architectural review process requires careful planning before refactoring: developers must outline the change scope, identify affected services, list required database migrations, and propose a rollback strategy. This planning phase should happen before Claude Code starts generating code. However, for routine changes like adding a new API endpoint to an existing service, you want Claude Code to execute immediately without the planning overhead. What mode should be applied in different scenarios?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Plan mode (--plan flag) for architectural refactors and multi-service changes; use direct execution mode (default) for routine endpoint additions. Configure this via .claude/rules/ glob patterns that route different paths to different modes
options.explanation: Plan mode gives the model time to reason through complex changes before execution. Direct mode is fast for routine work. Routing via rules based on directory patterns (e.g., services/*/api-endpoint.ts → direct) aligns with architecture. PLAN for complex, DIRECT for routine.

options.label: B
options.text: Always use Plan mode (--plan flag) for all work to ensure every change is carefully reviewed before execution, even for simple endpoint additions
options.explanation: Plan mode adds latency and planning overhead for routine changes that don't need it. Causes unnecessary delays for simple, low-risk operations. PLAN > DIRECT only when risk warrants it.

options.label: C
options.text: Always use direct execution mode to maximize speed and productivity; prevent Plan mode via CLAUDE_SKIP_PLANNING flag in settings.json
options.explanation: Direct mode for architectural refactors loses the benefit of careful planning and increases risk of mistakes. Also, CLAUDE_SKIP_PLANNING flag is fabricated. Doesn't exist.

options.label: D
options.text: Create .claude/commands/review-and-execute that prompts developers to manually approve changes before Claude Code runs them, regardless of change type
options.explanation: Manual approval for every change contradicts the goal of developer productivity. Also doesn't distinguish between routine vs architectural; treats them identically.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need different handling for different change types: routine (fast) vs architectural (careful). B all plan (too slow). C all direct (too risky), uses fabricated flag. D manual approval (contradicts productivity). A uses plan mode for complex, direct for routine, configured via rules → correct → select A.

questions.question: Your CI/CD pipeline uses Claude Code to auto-generate deployment configurations for staging and production environments. The pipeline is non-interactive (runs on a GitLab runner) and must not prompt for input or open the VS Code interface. You need to generate config files with environment-specific variables, run validations, and output results to job logs. The runner cannot access interactive terminals. Which flag configuration properly configures Claude Code for this CI/CD context?

options.label: A
options.text: Invoke Claude Code with the -p (--print) flag to output results to stdout and --non-interactive to suppress input prompts, ensuring all output is captured in job logs
options.explanation: -p flag directs output to stdout (captured by CI logs). --non-interactive prevents blocking on input prompts. Together, these enable fully automated CI/CD workflows. EXPLICIT FLAGS for CI/CD automation.

options.label: B
options.text: Invoke Claude Code with CLAUDE_CI_MODE environment variable set to true and the --batch flag to process multiple files
options.explanation: CLAUDE_CI_MODE and --batch flags do not exist. This is a plausible-sounding fabrication mimicking correct intent (automation) but using non-existent syntax.

options.label: C
options.text: Run Claude Code normally without flags, then pipe stdout to tee to capture output in job logs
options.explanation: Without -p flag, Claude Code's interactive interface may still attempt to open or prompt for input. Piping stdout doesn't solve the interactive prompting problem. The -p flag is specifically designed for this.

options.label: D
options.text: Use CLAUDE_HEADLESS environment variable to suppress the UI, ensuring the runner never attempts to open interface windows
options.explanation: CLAUDE_HEADLESS is a fabricated feature. The correct flag is -p (--print). Though the intent is correct, the implementation doesn't exist in Claude Code.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need non-interactive, non-prompted, output-to-logs behavior for CI/CD. B and D fabricated flags (eliminate). C without -p risks interactive behavior (weak). A uses -p (output to stdout) + --non-interactive (no prompts) → correct → select A.

questions.question: Your API server generates POST endpoints that must sanitize user input before database writes. A PostToolUse hook validates generated code by checking for sanitization calls, but currently it only detects exact function names (sanitize_input). When developers write custom sanitization functions with different names (cleanInput, validate, scrubData), the hook fails to validate them as legitimate. How should you improve the hook to recognize semantic equivalence without hardcoding every possible function name?

options.label: A
options.text: Update the PostToolUse hook to ask Claude Code for semantic code analysis: provide the generated function context and have it check whether any input transformation occurs before database calls, rather than pattern-matching specific function names
options.explanation: Semantic analysis via Claude Code is more robust than regex patterns. The hook can query Claude to understand function intent, not just match names. Enables custom sanitization functions without enumeration. CODE > NAME PATTERNS.

options.label: B
options.text: Maintain a blocklist of known dangerous functions like direct_to_db() and flag those in the hook; trust that other functions are safe
options.explanation: Blocklist approach is fragile — relies on enumerating all unsafe patterns. Misses novel unsafe patterns. Doesn't guarantee safe patterns; inverts the logic (should be allowlist for critical operations).

options.label: C
options.text: Create a .claude/rules/sanitization.yaml that lists all approved sanitization function names (sanitize_input, cleanInput, validate, scrubData, etc.) and blocks code that doesn't use one of them
options.explanation: Hardcoding approved names creates maintenance burden and blocks legitimate new functions. Also, this is a preventive rule, not a validation hook. Doesn't solve the specific problem of validating existing custom functions.

options.label: D
options.text: Disable the PostToolUse validation hook and rely on unit tests in the CI pipeline to catch missing sanitization, since hooks cannot semantically analyze code
options.explanation: Hooks require semantic analysis is false — the hook can delegate to Claude. Also, moving validation to tests loses immediate feedback during code generation. HOOKS > tests for real-time feedback.

questions.algorithmTrace: Problem: hook pattern-matches function names, misses semantic equivalent custom functions. B blocklist (fragile). C hardcoded list (maintenance burden, blocks new functions). D false claim hooks can't analyze semantically. A uses Claude semantic analysis in hook → correct → select A.

questions.question: You run parallel research agents that each generate analysis reports into shared directories at reports/{agent_id}/findings.md. The agents operate with independent Claude Code sessions. To prevent file corruption from simultaneous writes and preserve memory context across sessions, you want to configure separate memory spaces per agent while ensuring shared context (like methodology definitions) remains consistent across all memory scopes. What's the proper architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Use agent-specific memory at .claude/memory/{agent_id}/ for isolated session context, and shared memory at .claude/memory/shared/ for methodology definitions. Each agent session loads both scopes: local scope for current session, shared for reference
options.explanation: Memory is scopable: project (.claude/), session (in-memory), and custom paths. Agent-specific paths isolate state per agent; shared path provides consistency. Each session loads its scope + shared scope. Prevents corruption and maintains methodology coherence.

options.label: B
options.text: Use a single shared memory file at .claude/memory/research.md with locks to prevent concurrent writes; configure all agents to use the same file
options.explanation: File locks don't prevent corruption in concurrent agent scenarios reliably. Shared single file means all agent memory mingles, losing isolation. No per-agent context preservation.

options.label: C
options.text: Store all memory in the global ~/.claude/memory/ directory so all agents on the system can access it automatically
options.explanation: Global memory applies to all projects and agents, not scoped per-agent. Loses isolation between agents and between projects. Also, global scope is for personal preferences, not project-specific research.

options.label: D
options.text: Disable memory persistence and rely on in-session context only; use a separate PostgreSQL database to store findings between sessions
options.explanation: Disabling memory loses Claude Code context management benefits. External database solves storage but not the memory scope problem. Context continuity within Claude Code sessions requires memory configuration.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need: isolated memory per agent + shared reference data. B single file with locks (unreliable). C global scope (wrong scope, loses isolation). D disables memory (loses benefits). A agent-specific + shared scopes → correct → select A.

questions.question: Your extraction pipeline uses Claude Code to parse customer documents and generate JSON outputs. Some Claude Code steps may try to use prohibited tools (web_search, file_system_delete) that would disrupt the extraction workflow. You need to explicitly deny access to dangerous tools while allowing safe ones (file_read, json_parsing). Currently, you allow all tools by default. What's the most maintainable control strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Create .claude/settings.json with deniedTools: ['web_search', 'file_system_delete'] to explicitly blocklist dangerous operations; default allows remaining tools per CCA-F principle EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT
options.explanation: settings.json deniedTools list is explicit and maintainable. Blocklist dangerous tools, default-allow safe ones. Aligns with EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT — lists what's prohibited, not implied restrictions.

options.label: B
options.text: Create .claude/settings.json with allowedTools: ['file_read', 'json_parsing'] to allowlist only safe tools; deny everything else
options.explanation: Allowlist is overly restrictive and requires adding every new safe tool. If new safe tools are added in Claude Code updates, they're blocked until explicitly added. More maintenance burden than blocklist.

options.label: C
options.text: Create .claude/rules/extraction.yaml with a PreToolUse hook that examines each tool use request and prompts the developer for approval
options.explanation: PreToolUse hooks can validate but prompting for approval isn't scalable (stops automation). Also, hooks are for validation/transformation, not approval gates. Tool access control belongs in settings.json.

options.label: D
options.text: Use a CLAUDE_SAFE_MODE environment variable in the root CLAUDE.md file to automatically prevent unsafe tools
options.explanation: CLAUDE_SAFE_MODE is a fabricated feature. Tool access control is configured via .claude/settings.json, not environment variables or CLAUDE.md directives.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need explicit control over prohibited tools. B allowlist (over-restrictive, maintenance). C prompts (not scalable). D fabricated feature. A deniedTools in settings.json (explicit, maintainable, aligns with EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT) → select A.

questions.question: Your team manages three simultaneous feature branches using Git worktrees (main, feature/auth-refactor, feature/payments-v2).
