Each worktree has a different .claude/rules/ configuration: auth-refactor requires strict type safety rules, payments-v2 requires transaction safety rules, main has minimal rules. When a developer switches between worktrees using `git worktree list` and `git checkout`, Claude Code must load the correct rules for that worktree's context. How should this be configured?

options.label: A
options.text: Place .claude/rules/ at the root of each worktree directory. Claude Code automatically detects the current working directory's .claude/ configuration and loads rules specific to that worktree. No additional setup required
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md hierarchy loads greedily: walks up from current directory, stopping at first .claude/ found. Worktree root-level .claude/rules/ are automatically loaded for that worktree. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT — each worktree explicitly manages its rules.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a ~/.claude/worktree-config.yaml that maps each worktree path to its rules configuration, and configure Claude Code to read this mapping on startup
options.explanation: Personal ~/.claude/ scope applies globally, not per-worktree. Worktree-specific config belongs in project scope, not personal. Requires startup configuration each time. PROJECT SCOPE > PERSONAL SCOPE.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a single root-level .claude/rules/ that lists all rules for all branches (type-safety, transaction-safety, minimal) with conditional globs like branches/auth-refactor/** and branches/payments-v2/**
options.explanation: Single rules file loses flexibility and clarity. Hard to maintain as worktrees grow. Also, Git worktrees don't use branches/ directory structure. Conflates branch naming with filesystem structure.

options.label: D
options.text: Set CLAUDE_WORKTREE_MODE environment variable to 'auto-detect' and configure it in each worktree's CLAUDE.md environment block
options.explanation: CLAUDE_WORKTREE_MODE is a fabricated feature. Worktree detection is automatic via directory hierarchy. Environment variables in CLAUDE.md aren't the mechanism for rules loading.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need rules to switch when switching worktrees. B personal scope (wrong scope). C single file (inflexible, wrong structure). D fabricated feature. A .claude/rules/ at worktree root, auto-loaded by hierarchy → correct → select A.

questions.question: Your frontend code generator creates React components across multiple files: component.tsx, component.module.css, component.test.tsx, and index.ts. Currently, generated components have inconsistent import paths (relative vs aliased), style variable naming, and test structure. When Claude Code generates these four files simultaneously, they need to share consistent context (import conventions, style variable names, test patterns) to ensure cohesion. What's the best approach to maintain consistency across the multi-file generation?

options.label: A
options.text: Create .claude/rules/component-generation.yaml that applies to components/**/*.tsx and components/**/*.module.css with shared context definitions for import-path conventions and style naming; each file type's rules reference the shared context
options.explanation: Single rules file with glob patterns covering all generated file types ensures shared context. Rules can define conventions (imports, style names) once; all files generated simultaneously see the same definitions. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT maintains cohesion.

options.label: B
options.text: Create separate rules files (.claude/rules/tsx.yaml, .claude/rules/css.yaml, .claude/rules/tests.yaml) and ensure developers manually synchronize naming conventions across files
options.explanation: Multiple files with manual sync defeats automation. Errors occur when developer forgets to sync. Also: BUILT-IN > CUSTOM — rules should enforce, not require manual discipline.

options.label: C
options.text: Create .claude/commands/generate-component that developers invoke with a component name, which internally runs structured prompts to generate all four files with consistent context
options.explanation: Requires developer invocation and custom prompt management. Rules-based approach is more maintainable than prompt-based commands. Also, doesn't guarantee consistency if Claude Code is used separately to edit files later.

options.label: D
options.text: Use CLAUDE_COMPONENT_MODE multi-file-context flag in settings.json to enable context sharing across related files during simultaneous generation
options.explanation: CLAUDE_COMPONENT_MODE and multi-file-context flags are fabricated. Multi-file consistency is achieved via rules with shared context definitions, not special flags.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need consistency across 4 file types generated together. B manual sync (fails automation). C developer invocation (not automatic, prompt-based). D fabricated flag. A single rules file with globs + shared context → automatic, explicit, maintainable → select A.

questions.question: Your customer support team uses Claude Code both in VS Code IDE (for complex multi-file ticket resolutions) and in CI/CD terminal automation (for routine response generation). The IDE workflow requires interactive planning and exploration; the CI/CD workflow must be fully automated without prompts. However, you need consistent rules and memory across both environments. What configuration strategy handles both workflows without duplication?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure .claude/rules/ and .claude/memory/ at project root (shared by both IDE and CLI). Use --plan flag in IDE for interactive work, -p (--print) flag in CI/CD for automation. Both environments load the same rules and memory automatically via directory hierarchy
options.explanation: Project-scoped .claude/ applies to both IDE and CLI per directory hierarchy. Mode flags (--plan vs -p) differ per context but load same rules/memory. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT (flags clarify intent), PROJECT SCOPE > PERSONAL SCOPE.

options.label: B
options.text: Create separate ~/.claude/ide-config.yaml and ~/.claude/cli-config.yaml to maintain different configurations for each environment, preventing conflicts
options.explanation: Personal ~/.claude/ scope isn't environment-specific; applies globally. Creates duplication and maintenance burden. PROJECT SCOPE > PERSONAL SCOPE for shared work.

options.label: C
options.text: Set CLAUDE_ENVIRONMENT=IDE or CLAUDE_ENVIRONMENT=CI in each context's environment, and create conditional rules in .claude/rules/config.yaml that apply based on environment variable
options.explanation: Rules are executed based on glob patterns and file paths, not environment variables. Conditional rule logic doesn't exist in Claude Code rules. Also duplicates config instead of reusing shared rules.

options.label: D
options.text: Duplicate .claude/rules/ and .claude/memory/ in both IDE and CLI tooling directories to ensure each environment has independent configuration copies
options.explanation: Duplication creates consistency problems when rules or memory diverge. Changes must be made in two places. Violates DRY principle. PROJECT SCOPE hierarchy handles both automatically without duplication.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need same rules/memory for IDE and CLI, but different modes. B personal scope (wrong scope). C environment conditionals (don't exist). D duplication (maintenance horror). A project-scoped shared config + mode flags per context → correct → select A.

questions.question: You maintain a .claude/settings.json that configures tool access and behavior globally for the project. A new security requirement mandates that the passwords and secrets used to configure Claude Code (API credentials, environment keys) must be stored in a separate file that's never committed to Git, and must override the committed settings.json when present. What's the correct local configuration file strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Create .claude/settings.local.json with override values for sensitive fields (API keys, credentials). This file is listed in .gitignore. Claude Code loads both settings.json (committed) and settings.local.json (local only), with local values overriding committed ones
options.explanation: .claude/settings.local.json is the standard override mechanism for local/sensitive config. Automatically loaded after settings.json. Provides separation of committed vs local-only secrets. Aligns with .gitignore pattern for credentials.

options.label: B
options.text: Store secrets in ~/.claude/secrets.yaml (personal scope) and reference them in project settings.json using ${SECRET_NAME} variable interpolation
options.explanation: Personal scope secrets (~/.claude/) apply to all projects, not project-specific. Variable interpolation syntax doesn't exist in Claude Code. Also uses global scope instead of project-scoped local file.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a CLAUDE_CONFIG_OVERRIDE environment variable pointing to a local config file, and configure the root CLAUDE.md to load it on startup
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md doesn't have a config override directive. Environment variable approach is less maintainable than .claude/settings.local.json. Standard pattern is .local file, not custom env variable.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Git's credential.helper to store secrets externally and have Claude Code read credentials from the Git credential system
options.explanation: Git credentials are for Git auth, not Claude Code configuration. Claude Code doesn't read from Git credential system. Confuses tool-specific secrets with Git auth.

questions.algorithmTrace: Need local override of committed config for secrets. B personal scope (wrong scope), interpolation syntax (doesn't exist). C env variable approach (non-standard). D Git credentials (wrong system). A .claude/settings.local.json (standard override pattern, .gitignore, automatic loading) → select A.

questions.question: Your development team uses Claude to automatically review Terraform infrastructure code in pull requests. The system must enforce strict security policies: flag hardcoded secrets, require encryption on storage resources, and validate IAM policy scopes. Reviewers currently spend 15 minutes per PR clarifying ambiguous feedback. You need structured output—each issue must include: resource_type (string), issue_category (enum: secret|encryption|iam|policy), severity_level (enum: critical|high|medium), and remediation_steps (array). Which approach best ensures consistent, policy-compliant output across all 50+ daily PRs?

options.label: A
options.text: Use temperature 0 with a comprehensive system prompt that explicitly specifies output as JSON schema with required fields, acceptable enums, and validation rules. Include {properties: {resource_type, issue_category, severity_level, remediation_steps}, required: [...]} in prompt.
options.explanation: EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT and BUILT-IN > CUSTOM principles. Explicit schema in system prompt + temperature 0 ensures deterministic output format. No post-processing needed. Directly targets the pain point (15 min clarification).

options.label: B
options.text: Provide 8 examples of well-reviewed Terraform PRs with properly formatted JSON results, allowing the model to infer the output format.
options.explanation: Valid but implicit—examples don't guarantee format compliance. Adds token overhead. Schema enforcement is more reliable than format inference.

options.label: C
options.text: Ask reviewers to specify their preferred output format in each PR comment, allowing customization per request.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: moves burden to users, eliminates consistency across 50+ daily PRs, doesn't scale.

options.label: D
options.text: Use temperature 1.0 to maximize creativity in detecting security issues, with a note that formatting consistency is less important than finding vulnerabilities.
options.explanation: Plausible-but-wrong: high temperature causes inconsistent output. Security classification requires deterministic behavior (temp 0), not creative variance.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Requirement: consistent structured output, policy compliance, 50+ PRs/day. 2. Temperature choice: 0 ensures deterministic classification (security decisions need consistency). 3. Format specification: explicit schema in system prompt beats implicit learning from examples. 4. Eliminate options: B (implicit), C (shifts burden), D (wrong temperature direction). 5. Answer: A—EXPLICIT specification + temperature 0 + BUILT-IN schema enforcement.

questions.question: Your IDE plugin generates boilerplate React components with TypeScript. You recently added 12 well-annotated examples (useState, useEffect, error handling patterns) to the system prompt. Telemetry shows: token count +28%, latency +180ms, but quality improvement +3.2%. The plugin serves 15K requests daily, and users report noticeable slowdown. Your cost is $1200/month. What's the optimal action?

options.label: A
options.text: Remove most examples—keep only 1-2 high-quality reference examples and rely on model's pre-trained knowledge instead. Sacrifice 3% quality for 28% token reduction and 180ms latency win.
options.explanation: COST-AWARE principle: few-shot learning shows diminishing returns (3.2% gain for 28% token overhead). 1-2 examples retain signal while cutting fat. 15K requests × 28% savings = significant UX improvement per user.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep all 12 examples; customers will adapt to slower IDE responsiveness eventually.
options.explanation: Ignores measurable customer pain (180ms latency). IDE tools compete on speed; user adaptation timeouts are migration risk.

options.label: C
options.text: Add even more examples (18+) to reach higher quality; cost and latency are acceptable for premium tooling.
options.explanation: Worsens the problem. Law of diminishing returns: 12→18 examples won't yield more than 3% additional gain. Escalates cost.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep all 12 examples but switch to Batch API to hide latency and reduce cost 50%.
options.explanation: Misapplies Batch API: it cuts cost but increases latency further (24h SLA for IDE generation isn't realistic). Doesn't solve real-time user experience.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Problem: token overhead 28%, latency +180ms, quality gain only 3.2%. 2. Cost-benefit: is 3.2% quality worth 28% tokens + 180ms delay? No. 3. Diminishing returns: examples 1-2 carry most signal; 12 is redundant. 4. Trade-off principle: COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE (remove fat examples). 5. Eliminate D (Batch API wrong tool for real-time); eliminate B (ignores UX); eliminate C (escalates problem). 6. Answer: A—keep minimal examples.

questions.question: Your fintech SaaS extracts structured data from grant applications: {applicant_name (string), entity_type (enum: sole_proprietor|llc|c_corp|nonprofit), funding_requested (integer, cents), description (string), approval_probability (float 0-1)}. You process 500 applications daily. Currently Claude outputs quasi-JSON or markdown tables, requiring manual validation. Post-processing introduces 14% error rate and $900/month in manual labor. Last month you tried JSON examples but errors only dropped to 12%. What's the most reliable approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement JSON schema validation in Claude API call (parameter: response_format with schema definition). This guarantees syntactically-valid JSON output, eliminating the entire post-processing error class.
options.explanation: BUILT-IN > CUSTOM. Native schema enforcement is guaranteed (model can't deviate syntax). Eliminates 14% error class entirely. Ends manual labor cost. Not dependent on examples.

options.label: B
options.text: Add 15 annotated grant applications showing exact JSON structure, hoping to improve format adherence.
options.explanation: Valid-but-suboptimal: examples already tried (12% error rate). Schema enforcement > format examples for hard constraints.

options.label: C
options.text: Require applicants to submit as pre-formatted JSON files instead of PDFs or forms.
options.explanation: Shifts burden to users (applicant friction), not scalable, doesn't solve the underlying output format problem.

options.label: D
options.text: Manually validate and fix malformed output before storing in database—hire part-time contractor at $2K/month.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: more expensive than current ($900→$2000), doesn't scale (500/day × 12% = 60 daily failures), doesn't solve root cause.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Current state: 14% post-processing error rate, $900/month cost, examples didn't help (12% still). 2. Root cause: model can generate invalid JSON syntax. 3. Example-based improvements maxed out at 12%. Need different lever. 4. BUILT-IN > CUSTOM: schema enforcement is native feature, eliminates syntax variance entirely. 5. Eliminate B (examples already tried); eliminate C (user friction); eliminate D (cost + scale issue). 6. Answer: A—schema enforcement.

questions.question: Your research synthesis platform uses Claude to consolidate findings from 15-25 research papers into 3000-word literature reviews. Current output lacks reasoning transparency—reviewers repeatedly ask 'Why prioritize this source?' and 'How do contradictory studies relate?' Enabling extended thinking (15K token budget) improved quality 38% (better source weighting, explicit contradiction discussion). Monthly volume: 180 reviews for academic customers who value rigor over speed. Extended thinking increases latency 240s and cost 32%. Current spend: $3400/month. Should you enable extended thinking globally?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes. Enable extended thinking by default with explicit 15K token budget limit. 38% quality gain justifies 32% cost increase for research workloads where truth-seeking > speed. Cost becomes $4488/month—acceptable for academic segment.
options.explanation: COST-AWARE principle within domain context: research workloads prioritize rigor. 38% quality improvement is significant. 32% cost increase ($1088) spread across 180 reviews = $6/review premium for quality. Academic customers expect/accept this trade-off.

options.label: B
options.text: Never use extended thinking; always prioritize speed over quality. Route reasoning-intensive reviews to manual analysts instead.
options.explanation: Too dogmatic. Extended thinking directly improves the automated output quality to near-manual level. Manual routing defeats the purpose.

options.label: C
options.text: Only enable extended thinking when customers explicitly request it via checkbox—let them opt-in to the cost.
options.explanation: Valid but misses value: most customers won't opt-in without understanding benefit. Default-on + transparency is better than forcing opting-in. For this segment, quality is the default expectation.

options.label: D
options.text: Use extended thinking but compensate by batching 50 reviews per Batch API request to amortize token cost.
options.explanation: Misapplies tool: Batch API reduces per-request cost but doesn't change extended thinking token budget; still $4488/month. Batching won't reduce the 15K-token extended thinking per review.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Use case: research synthesis, academic customers, 180 reviews/month. 2. Quality improvement: 38% is transformative (explicit reasoning, contradiction handling). 3. Cost analysis: $1088 increase on $3400 base = 32%, ~$6/review. 4. Segment fit: academic research prioritizes rigor > speed. Default-on is appropriate. 5. Eliminate B (too dogmatic); eliminate C (misses opt-in/transparency dynamics); eliminate D (batching doesn't reduce extended thinking cost). 6. Answer: A—enable by default.

questions.question: Your SaaS customer support AI handles refund requests. Policy states: 'Refund granted for defective product within 30 days; no exceptions.' But edge cases exist (account sabotage at day 45, wrong product shipped on day 28). Currently temperature 1.0 + top_p 0.9. Reviewers see inconsistency: sometimes identical situations get opposite decisions. Other times the AI approves refunds for cosmetic issues outside the window. You need consistency on policy boundaries but recognition of legitimate exceptions. What settings best balance both needs?

options.label: A
options.text: Reduce temperature to 0.3 and top_p to 0.8. Lower temperature enforces decision consistency on policy boundaries (30-day window, defect classification). Model still recognizes exceptions through explicit context, not temperature variance.
options.explanation: EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT: lower temp+top_p enforce policy logic deterministically. Edge cases are handled by prompt context (instructions for exceptions) not by temperature variance. Best of both: consistency + nuance.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep temperature 1.0 but increase top_p to 0.99 to activate more diverse reasoning paths and capture edge cases.
options.explanation: Worsens consistency: higher settings increase variance in policy boundary decisions. Edge case handling should come from prompt clarity, not temperature.

options.label: C
options.text: Use temperature 0 with top_p 0.5—eliminate all variability to ensure pure policy adherence.
options.explanation: Over-corrects: temperature 0 + restrictive top_p prevents any nuanced edge case judgment. Needs some flexibility for legitimate exceptions (sabotage scenario).

options.label: D
options.text: Use temperature 1.5 and top_p 0.95 to maximize edge case detection and creative exception thinking.
options.explanation: Contradicts requirements: higher temperature worsens consistency exactly where needed (policy boundaries). 'Creative' decisions on refunds = risk.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Requirements: consistent policy boundary enforcement + recognition of edge cases. 2. Root cause: temperature 1.0 creates variance in both areas. 3. Solution: lower temp/top_p for consistency + explicit prompt for edge case instructions. 4. Eliminate D (creative/high temp worsens consistency); eliminate B (increases variance); eliminate C (too rigid, blocks nuance). 5. Answer: A—temperature 0.3 + 0.8 top_p for consistency; prompt clarity for exceptions.

questions.question: Your code documentation assistant caches an 8KB technical API reference document as a static prompt prefix for all requests. Users ask questions about the API; each question adds 2KB of query + code context. You process 1.2M API calls/month. Cache hit rate is 96% (documentation extremely relevant). Documentation updates quarterly on fixed schedule. What is the optimal cache strategy given these constraints?

options.label: A
options.text: Cache the entire 8KB documentation as one monolithic static block. Invalidate on schedule (quarterly on first Monday). Maximizes hit rate (96%) and token savings across all requests.
options.explanation: COST-AWARE + STATIC-CONTENT-FIRST principles. Monolithic static cache = highest hit rate (96%). Quarterly invalidation = predictable refresh. Simple architecture. 1.2M calls × 96% hit rate × cached tokens = maximum savings.

options.label: B
options.text: Split documentation into 1KB subsections by API endpoint (50+ chunks). Users can request specific sections, improving targeting.
options.explanation: Fragmented cache defeats purpose: hit rate fragments too (user likely needs multiple sections). Adds complexity without benefit. Monolithic static cache is simpler.

options.label: C
options.text: Don't cache documentation; ask users to paste relevant sections in their query if needed.
options.explanation: Eliminates entire savings opportunity. 1.2M calls × average 8KB doc = massive token waste. Shifts burden to users (poor UX).

options.label: D
options.text: Cache only 2KB of code examples dynamically; re-request full documentation each call.
options.explanation: Misses major savings (6KB uncached). 'Dynamic' cache defeats the point (documentation is static). Breaks cache logic.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Assets: 8KB static doc, 2KB dynamic query, 1.2M calls/month, 96% hit rate. 2. Cache strategy: maximize hits with static-first principle. 3. Monolithic vs fragmented: 96% hit rate suggests docs are comprehensively needed → monolithic better. 4. Invalidation: quarterly schedule = predictable, avoids stale cache. 5. Eliminate C (no caching); eliminate D (loses savings); eliminate B (fragments hits). 6. Answer: A—monolithic static cache, quarterly invalidation.

questions.question: Your nightly CI/CD pipeline analyzes 2200 test execution logs, extracting root causes for each failure. Currently it uses synchronous API calls: $650/month, 2.5-hour processing window. Each analysis is independent (zero inter-dependencies). You estimated switch to Batch API: 50% cost savings ($325/month), 24-hour SLA instead of real-time. Team discussed waiting until results are guaranteed same-day, but 24h is technically 'next business day.' Is Batch API appropriate for this workload and when should you switch?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, switch immediately. 50% savings ($325/month), 24h SLA matches nightly batch processing perfectly. Results before next business day start is acceptable for non-urgent analysis logs.
options.explanation: Batch API design intent: exactly this scenario (high-volume, cost-sensitive, non-time-sensitive). 24h SLA = results before next day start. $325/month savings × 12 = $3900 annual. No blocking reason to wait.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep synchronous API; test log analysis needs real-time availability in case of urgent incident triaging.
options.explanation: False premise: logs are analyzed nightly (not incident-time). Incident response needs happen in parallel, not blocked on batch results.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Batch API but aggregate all 2200 logs into a single API request to maximize token savings.
options.explanation: Misunderstands Batch API: batching doesn't require aggregating data into one request (that would use more tokens). Batch batches independent requests, not payloads.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Batch API only on Friday nights to process weekend backlogs; keep synchronous during weekdays.
options.explanation: Arbitrary split: Batch API is cost-optimized regardless of day. Hybrid approach adds complexity without benefit. If 24h SLA works, it works every day.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Workload: 2200 independent analyses, nightly batch. 2. Batch API preconditions: non-latency-sensitive (✓), high-volume (✓), cost-sensitive (✓). 3. SLA fit: 24h for next-morning results = acceptable for batch processing. 4. ROI: $325 monthly savings is significant. 5. Eliminate B (false premise on urgency); eliminate C (misunderstands batching); eliminate D (arbitrary scheduling). 6. Answer: A—switch immediately.

questions.question: Your code-gen platform generates 450-500 line Python modules. Current prompt is 3.2KB: 48 small code examples + 2KB system instructions + 1.2KB guidelines + 0.5KB constraints. Output quality is consistently high. Monthly spend: $8900. You analyzed token logs: 30 of 48 examples are near-duplicates (similar patterns, mostly cosmetic differences). Hypothesis: removing them saves 1.25KB per request (350 tokens). For 110K monthly requests at $0.30/1M tokens, what's the ROI of removing redundant examples?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, remove the 30 redundant examples. Savings: 1.25KB × 110K requests = ~137.5M tokens monthly = ~$4.1K/month reduction (46% cost cut). Validate 1% quality regression first; if minimal, implement immediately.
options.explanation: COST-AWARE + data-driven. Few-shot learning shows diminishing returns (duplicate examples add minimal signal). Quantified ROI: 46% cost reduction. Contingent validation step keeps risk low.

options.label: B
options.text: Never remove examples; they are the foundation of output quality and we should invest more, not cut.
options.explanation: Dogmatic: redundancy ≠ value. Duplicate examples don't improve quality proportionally—classic diminishing returns.

options.label: C
options.text: Remove all 48 examples at once to maximize savings (would save ~$6800/month).
options.explanation: Too aggressive: while 30 are redundant, the remaining 18 have signal. Complete removal risks quality cliff. Surgical removal is safer.

options.label: D
options.text: Compress all examples into pseudocode/abbreviated form to save tokens without removing them.
options.explanation: Doesn't help: pseudocode still requires tokens to encode. Compression doesn't reduce token count meaningfully for small optimizations.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Redundancy identified: 30/48 examples near-duplicate. 2. Savings calculation: 1.25KB × 110K = 137.5M tokens × $0.30/1M = $41/K tokens. 3. ROI: $4.1K/month = 46% cost reduction, ~$49K annually. 4. Few-shot curve: duplicate examples have minimal marginal value. 5. Risk mitigation: validate quality first. 6. Eliminate B (ignores diminishing returns); eliminate C (too aggressive); eliminate D (compression ineffective). 7. Answer: A—surgical removal with validation.

questions.question: Your support chatbot gathers customer context via multi-turn conversation: Turn 1 collects problem description (200 tokens), Turn 2 gets error logs (400 tokens), Turn 3 elicits attempted solutions (300 tokens), then generates diagnosis (1KB output). This conversational flow builds rapport and feels natural. Alternative: one long initial prompt asking all 3 pieces upfront (900 tokens in chatbot-to-model call). Multi-turn costs 3 API calls (slightly more latency), one-turn costs 1 call. Which design optimizes for both UX and efficiency while preserving conversation quality?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep multi-turn conversation structure for UX/rapport, but use prompt caching: cache the accumulated context from Turns 1-2 before Turn 3. Eliminates re-sending 600 tokens, preserves natural flow.
options.explanation: EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT (maintains conversation flow) + BUILT-IN tools (caching). Caching solves efficiency without UX sacrifice. Conversation context accumulates naturally, cache checkpoint prevents re-processing.

options.label: B
options.text: Switch to single-prompt design asking all 3 pieces upfront; accept the loss of conversational UX for 1-call efficiency.
options.explanation: Valid-but-suboptimal: saves 2 API calls but loses rapport and context-building. Conversation is valuable for support quality.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep multi-turn but ask for all information in Turn 1 to maximize cache hit rates.
options.explanation: Defeats purpose: asking for everything at once isn't a multi-turn conversation (it's just one turn split into label). Loses the UX benefit entirely.

options.label: D
options.text: Two completely separate systems: multi-turn for conversational UI, single-prompt API for batch operations. No middle ground.
options.explanation: False dichotomy: caching provides the middle ground. Over-engineering.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Trade-off: multi-turn UX vs single-turn efficiency. 2. Constraints: rapport matters for support quality, efficiency matters for cost. 3. Solution: caching preserves both (builds conversation naturally + eliminates re-sending). 4. Cache checkpoint: after Turn 2 (600 tokens cached), Turn 3 only adds 300 new tokens. 5. Eliminate B (trades UX); eliminate C (not multi-turn); eliminate D (false dichotomy). 6. Answer: A—multi-turn + caching.

questions.question: Your invoice extraction pipeline processes 9000 invoices daily: {invoice_number, vendor, amount, date}. Validation catches ~180 malformed outputs (2% error rate). Malformed invoices feed to error queue; SLA requires reprocessing within 2 hours. Re-running failed invoice through the model with retry logic costs 40% more per request (warmer temperature, looser constraints to 'try different approach'). Monthly cost today: $4200. Should you implement targeted retry logic on just the 2% failures or apply stricter upfront rules to all 9000?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement validation gates + selective retry: let 98% succeed on first pass with standard settings, retry only the 180 failures with adjusted temperature (0.2) and stricter schema enforcement. Cost: ~$4350/month (only 2% pay retry premium).
options.explanation: COST-AWARE principle: separate success path from error path. 98% × standard cost + 2% × retry cost. Retry with **lower** temperature (not higher) improves format compliance. Efficient error isolation.

options.label: B
options.text: Apply stricter settings upfront to all 9000 invoices daily—lower temperature, strict schema—to achieve 100% first-pass accuracy.
options.explanation: Over-engineering: 98% already succeed. Strict settings add latency/cost/chance of false rejections on valid invoices. Wrong ratio of trade-off.

options.label: C
options.text: Accept 2% error rate as normal; don't retry—escalate failures to human data entry team instead.
options.explanation: Violates SLA: 180/day × 2hr SLA = 15 humans/shift minimum. Cost >> $4200. Doesn't scale.

options.label: D
options.text: Retry failures with temperature 1.8 to generate more diverse output attempts and find correct format.
options.explanation: Opposite of solution: higher temperature worsens JSON malformation. Diversity isn't helpful for structure format; precision is.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Status: 2% error rate (180/9000), 98% success on first pass. 2. Retry cost: 40% premium = $168/month to retry all 180. 3. Cost comparison: upfront strictness (affects all 9000) vs targeted retry (affects 180). 4. Solution: validation gates + selective retry with **lower** temperature improves format. 5. Eliminate B (over-applies strictness); eliminate C (scaling failure); eliminate D (wrong temperature direction). 6. Answer: A—validation + selective retry.

questions.question:
Your IDE code-completion plugin generates TypeScript function bodies starting with 'Here's your function:' followed by markdown code block. Current output: ~8% of responses lack closing backticks or have improper formatting, requiring client-side regex fixes. User impact: momentary visual glitch as client repairs syntax highlighting. You want zero formatting issues without adding validation logic. Response currently structured as: assistant message = 'Here's your function: 
```typescript
[GENERATED CODE]
```'. What change guarantees correct format from first token?

options.label: A
options.text: Prefill the assistant response to start with exactly '```typescript\n' and end with '\n```'. Model generates only the code body between markers. Forces format from token 1; zero formatting edge cases.
options.explanation: EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT and BUILT-IN > CUSTOM. Prefilling constrains output tokens from the very first generation. Eliminates entire class of formatting variance. Zero validation needed. Native prompt engineering solution.

options.label: B
options.text: Add 5-10 good examples of well-formatted code blocks in the system prompt.
options.explanation: Valid-but-suboptimal: examples are implicit. Still 8% failure rate expected; doesn't remove the edge case.

options.label: C
options.text: Post-process all responses with regex: match code blocks and fix missing backticks.
options.explanation: Anti-pattern: adds client-side latency, fragile regex approach to a deterministic format problem. Fixing symptoms, not root cause.

options.label: D
options.text: Increase temperature to 1.2 to encourage more varied and creative code formatting.
options.explanation: Plausible-but-wrong: higher temperature **worsens** format consistency (opposite effect needed). Creativity isn't the goal here; precision is.

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Problem: 8% formatting errors (missing backticks, glitched blocks). 2. Root cause: model can choose to include/exclude delimiters. 3. Solution: prefilling removes the choice—format is determined before generation starts. 4. Cost: zero (prefilling is prompt engineering, no latency). 5. Eliminate B (implicit examples); eliminate C (post-processing); eliminate D (higher temp worsens issue). 6. Answer: A—prefill format strings.

questions.question: Your research platform receives conflicting instructions from different stakeholders: Marketing wants 'maximum 3000 words' (keep it scannable); Research team insists 'include all major findings without omission.' For a complex machine-learning-safety topic with 35-40 major findings, fitting everything into 3000 words (8 words/finding) is physically impossible. The system currently prioritizes the word limit, stakeholders complain important research is missing. How should the system handle this structural conflict to maximize stakeholder trust and output usefulness?

options.label: A
options.text: Detect conflict upfront; prompt Claude with explicit structure: {conflicting_constraints: bool, resolved_choice: string, rationale: string}. Return structured explanation of which constraint won, why, and what was sacrificed. Make the trade-off transparent.
options.explanation: EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT principle. Structured output makes Claude's reasoning visible. Transparency builds trust (stakeholders understand the constraint was irreconcilable, not overlooked). Accounts for trade-off explicitly.

options.label: B
options.text: Ignore the word limit and include all findings; remove the 3000-word constraint from the prompt entirely.
options.explanation: Violates Marketing's requirement. Doesn't resolve conflict; just picks one side.

options.label: C
options.text: Split output: executive summary (3000 words, major findings) + detailed appendix (all 35-40 findings in full). Satisfies both constraints pragmatically.
options.explanation: Valid-but-suboptimal: it does solve the problem technically. But assumes appendix isn't bounded; if appendix is unbounded, it might violate spirit of compact deliverable. A's transparency approach builds more stakeholder buy-in.

options.label: D
options.text: Use extended thinking (25K token budget) to compress 35-40 findings into 3000 words through extreme synthesis; find a way to fit everything.
options.explanation: Misapplies tool: extended thinking doesn't magically make impossible constraints solvable. Token budget doesn't change word limits. Avoids the real problem (stakeholder misalignment).

questions.algorithmTrace: 1. Constraints: 3000 words vs all 40 findings (impossible ratio). 2. Current behavior: prioritizes one, stakeholders unhappy. 3. CCA-F principle: EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT—surface the conflict. 4. Options C vs A: C solves technically, A solves stakeholder-trust-wise. 5. A is better aligned with CCA-F values (transparency in architectural decisions). 6. Eliminate B (one-sided); eliminate D (doesn't solve constraint problem); eliminate C (valid but less aligned with EXPLICIT principle). 7. Answer: A—explicit conflict resolution.

questions.question: A customer support agent handles warranty claim requests. Average conversations run 35-40 exchanges covering product details, purchase history, damage assessment, and claim reasoning. After approximately 120K tokens (around exchange 28), telemetry shows that the agent begins repeating eligibility questions already answered in exchanges 4-7, contradicts damage assessments from exchanges 10-12, and loses track of claim amounts mentioned in exchanges 6 and 18. Resolution satisfaction drops from 87% to 52% after this point. The engineering team must maintain consistency throughout extended claims conversations without losing critical details. What approach best addresses this context degradation?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement a programmatic context summarization that auto-compacts conversation history into structured facts (product model, damage type, claim amount, assessment date) before token count exceeds 100K, with older exchanges archived and referenced via summary, not full replay
options.explanation: Explicit programmatic summarization is CODE > PROMPTS. It creates deterministic handoff points (not relying on the model to self-manage), maintains structured facts (not hoping the model remembers), and prevents context window saturation. Auto-triggering at 100K ensures proactive management before degradation starts.

options.label: B
options.text: Increase the system prompt emphasis on not repeating questions and maintaining consistency, with few-shot examples of multi-turn conversations where the agent tracks all details perfectly
options.explanation: Prompt-based guidance is probabilistic. The model 'knows' it should be consistent but still fails at 120K tokens. Few-shot examples add token overhead without solving the fundamental limit problem. IMPLICIT instructions fail under load.

options.label: C
options.text: Split long conversations into multiple shorter sessions, restarting the agent every 20 exchanges with a handoff summary passed to the next session
options.explanation: This is valid but suboptimal. Session boundaries create artificial friction and potential information loss at handoffs. It feels like giving up on context management rather than managing it properly. Requires explicit orchestration at every boundary.

options.label: D
options.text: Monitor the agent's self-reported confidence scores on factual accuracy and automatically escalate to a human when confidence drops below 75%
options.explanation: LLM self-reported confidence is unreliable and often inversely correlated with actual accuracy. At 120K tokens, the model may still sound confident while contradicting itself. Confidence is not a reliable escalation signal—content validation is.

questions.algorithmTrace: Eliminate D immediately: LLM confidence is unreliable anti-pattern. B is prompt-only, not deterministic under load. C is valid but shifts burden to orchestration. A is programmatic with explicit summarization triggers. CODE > PROMPTS AND COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE (summarization is cheaper than context stuffing). Select A.

questions.question: A code generation agent assists developers with incremental builds across 15-20 message exchanges per feature. In round 12-14, the agent proposes type definitions that contradict interfaces defined in exchanges 3 and 5, suggests module imports that would create circular dependencies (first mentioned as anti-patterns in exchange 7), and duplicates helper functions that were already written in exchanges 8-9. Testing then catches 18 integration failures per 100 feature requests. Developers report losing confidence around message 10. What strategy best preserves architectural consistency without restarting conversations?

options.label: A
options.text: Maintain an explicit architectural state document that tracks: (1) defined types and interfaces, (2) import graph and circular dependency risks, (3) implemented helpers with signatures. Update it after each code generation exchange and inject it as a structured reference before new proposals.
options.explanation: This is programmatic context management with explicit facts. CODE > PROMPTS. Instead of hoping the model remembers, you maintain a queryable state document. You inject facts that matter. This scales to any conversation length.

options.label: B
options.text: Add detailed few-shot examples to the system prompt showing the agent successfully tracking types and dependencies across 15-message conversations
options.explanation: Few-shot helps but is still prompt-guided. It adds tokens without addressing the scaling problem. At message 18, even good examples don't prevent the model from losing track of exchange 3.

options.label: C
options.text: Insert regular 'architecture checkpoint' messages where the agent is asked to summarize all types, imports, and helper functions it has seen so far, then validate this summary against the actual code
options.explanation: This is partially valid—it creates explicit handoff points. But it makes the agent summarize rather than having your system maintain the truth. The model's summary can still be wrong, and you're relying on it to stay accurate.

options.label: D
options.text: Use Claude's built-in context-tracking capability by enabling conversation continuity in the API, which internally manages context window usage automatically
options.explanation: Claude is a stateless LLM; context continuity is not automatic. API calls are independent. The model has no built-in awareness of conversation length limits or degradation patterns. You must implement context management explicitly.

questions.algorithmTrace: D is factually wrong—dismiss. B is prompt-only. C shifts burden to the agent's summarization (probabilistic). A maintains ground truth in a structured document, injected deterministically. A is CODE > PROMPTS. Select A.

questions.question: A multi-agent research system coordinates 4 specialized sub-agents: literature search, data analysis, synthesis, and review. The literature agent finds 120 relevant papers; the data agent computes 8 statistical summaries; the synthesis agent combines results into 6 hypothesis sections; the review agent validates each section. During synthesis (step 3), one data summary was accidentally marked as 'SKIPPED' instead of 'COMPLETED' due to a network timeout. The synthesis agent silently incorporated placeholder text where data was missing. The review agent flagged the error, but by then the malformed output was already cached and reused in three downstream reports. What change prevents this failure mode?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement explicit error objects with propagation rules: if any required upstream result has status 'SKIPPED' or 'FAILED', downstream agents must not proceed silently but must either return a structured error object, wait for retry, or escalate to a coordinator
options.explanation: Structured error propagation prevents silent failures. CODE > PROMPTS. Instead of hoping agents notice missing data, you make failures explicit and mandatory. Error objects flow through the pipeline. Caching respects error states (don't cache failures). Anti-pattern: silent failures or error suppression.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a validation step after synthesis where the agent checks that all required data summaries are present and warns if any are 'SKIPPED', but proceeds anyway if at least 75% are complete
options.explanation: This creates a warning but still proceeds silently. The 75% threshold is arbitrary and doesn't address the problem: malformed output still enters the cache and corrupts downstream work.

options.label: C
options.text: Disable caching for agent outputs until the review stage validates them, then cache only after validation passes
options.explanation: This is valid but suboptimal. It adds latency and review overhead. It's a workaround that treats caching as optional rather than making the pipeline itself reliable.

options.label: D
options.text: Use sentiment analysis of the synthesis agent's output to detect confidence drops that might indicate missing data, and automatically re-run the data agent if confidence is low
options.explanation: Sentiment/confidence analysis is unreliable (anti-pattern). The synthesis agent may sound extremely confident while building on malformed data. This is the inverse of useful: highest risk when agent doesn't signal doubt.

questions.algorithmTrace: D uses unreliable confidence signal—eliminate. B proceeds anyway (silent failure). C avoids caching but doesn't fix the pipeline. A makes errors explicit and mandatory to propagate. Structured error objects enforce deterministic handling. Select A.

questions.question: A code refactoring agent attempts to call external linting APIs that fail 3-5% of the time due to transient network issues. When a lint call fails, the agent either retries immediately (causing cascading failures and burning 15 tokens per retry) or gives up and continues with unvalidated code suggestions. Failed requests add ~800ms per attempt. End-to-end refactoring requests timeout 8% of the time when they hit a failing linker, leaving developers waiting 45+ seconds for no result. The team wants both reliability and cost efficiency. What approach best balances these constraints?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement exponential backoff (first retry after 50ms, second after 100ms, third after 200ms, max 3 retries) with a circuit breaker that disables lint validation temporarily if failure rate exceeds 10% over the last 50 requests, allowing graceful degradation to unvalidated suggestions
options.explanation: This is COST-AWARE reliability. Exponential backoff prevents hammer-drilling on a failing service. Circuit breaker stops wasting tokens when the service is unhealthy. Graceful degradation keeps the agent responsive even under transient failure. Couples retry strategy with cost awareness.

options.label: B
options.text: Always retry failed lint requests up to 10 times with a fixed 500ms delay between retries, ensuring the lint validation eventually succeeds
options.explanation: This is brute-force and expensive. BRUTE-FORCE > COST-AWARE is backwards. 10 retries with fixed delays burns tokens, increases timeout risk, and doesn't address root cause (transient service failures may persist).

options.label: C
options.text: Skip lint validation on first attempt; only call the lint API if the agent detects it has made a risky change (e.g., modifying error handling or database calls)
options.explanation: This is selective retries but still doesn't solve transient failures intelligently. Risk-based skipping leaves you vulnerable to bugs in 'safe' refactorings. Doesn't implement proper failure handling.

options.label: D
options.text: Cache lint results locally for 10 minutes so that repeated requests for the same code snippet don't hit the external API
options.explanation: Caching helps but doesn't address the original problem: transient API failures. If the API failed 3 minutes ago, the cache may contain stale results. Doesn't solve the reliability/cost tradeoff.

questions.algorithmTrace: C is selective but unreliable. D doesn't address transient failures. B is brute-force (expensive, anti-pattern). A combines exponential backoff (smart cost management) + circuit breaker (failure resilience) + graceful degradation (service availability). COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE. Select A.

questions.question: A support agent handles billing dispute escalations. Of the last 500 cases: 47 were incorrectly resolved by the agent alone (refund errors, policy misinterpretations), 23 escalated to humans but with incomplete context (agent didn't document reasoning), 12 escalated based on the agent's stated confidence score being 'low' but actually involved trivial issues, and 418 were resolved correctly without escalation. The business rule is: escalate cases involving refunds >$500 OR where the policy interpretation is ambiguous. Current escalation rates (38%) feel high, but incident rates (9.4%) are unacceptable. What change is most aligned with CCA-F principles?

options.label: A
options.text: Define explicit, programmatic escalation rules: IF (refund_amount > 500) OR (policy_tokens == 'ambiguous') THEN escalate_required=true. Remove agent confidence scoring from escalation logic entirely. Require structured context (case_id, reason_code, disputed_amount, policy_reference) before escalation.
options.explanation: This is EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT. Escalation rules are now programmatic and deterministic, not probabilistic. Policy ambiguity is flagged structurally, not relying on agent judgment. Confidence scores are removed (unreliable anti-pattern). Structured context ensures escalations are actionable. CODE > PROMPTS.

options.label: B
options.text: Train the agent with more few-shot examples of correct escalation decisions, emphasizing the refund threshold and policy boundary cases
options.explanation: This is IMPLICIT. Few-shot examples don't enforce rules; they guide probabilistically. The agent may still miscalculate refund amounts or misinterpret policies. Training doesn't eliminate the agent's unreliable self-assessment.

options.label: C
options.text: Lower the agent's confidence threshold for escalation from 'low' to 'medium', so that more ambiguous cases are escalated earlier
options.explanation: This doubles down on unreliable confidence signals. The real problem is that confidence is not a good escalation signal at all (anti-pattern). Tuning the threshold doesn't fix the fundamental issue.

options.label: D
options.text: Use sentiment analysis of the customer's messages to detect frustration, and automatically escalate if sentiment negative + agent confidence is low
options.explanation: Sentiment-based escalation is an anti-pattern. Customer frustration is often legitimate even for straightforward issues, and calm customers can have complex disputes. This conflates policy requirements with emotional signals.

questions.algorithmTrace: D uses sentiment (anti-pattern—unreliable). C tunes confidence threshold (still unreliable). B is prompt-based guidance (implicit, probabilistic). A defines explicit programmatic rules, removes confidence entirely, requires structured context. EXPLICIT > IMPLICIT, CODE > PROMPTS. Select A.

questions.question: A data extraction pipeline uses Claude to parse 2000+ invoices per day from diverse vendors. Raw cached prompt completions for the 'invoice header' section (vendor name, date, total amount) are reused across ~500 structurally identical invoices, saving 45% of token costs with 92% cache hit rates. However, every 3 days, a bulk client updates their invoice template (new field positions, color scheme, date format), and the cached assumptions become incorrect. Extraction accuracy drops from 97% to 43% for that cohort until someone notices and clears the cache (usually 4-6 hours later, 600-1200 malformed records). What mechanism prevents this systematically?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement semantic versioning of invoice templates: assign each vendor a template_version based on structure metadata (detected field positions, date formats, total line count). Cache keys include template_version. When a vendor's structure changes, template_version increments automatically, invalidating old cached results for that vendor without clearing global cache.
options.explanation: This is smart cache invalidation, not blind TTL. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE. You maintain 95% cache hits for stable vendors while automatically detecting when one vendor's structure changes. Semantic versioning prevents the 4-6 hour lag. Progressive validation (check template_version before cache lookup).

options.label: B
options.text: Implement a 6-hour TTL (time-to-live) on all cached invoice results, forcing fresh extraction if cache entries are older than 6 hours
options.explanation: Blind TTL is a band-aid. You're forcing 6-hour staleness windows and losing cache benefits for many vendors. Misses the actual problem: structure changes happen at unpredictable intervals, not on a clock.

options.label: C
options.text: Disable caching entirely for invoice extraction and rely on token optimization (compression, summarization) to reduce costs
options.explanation: Throwing away 45% cost savings is waste. Caching is valuable when managed properly. This is giving up on caching rather than fixing cache invalidation.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a post-extraction validation step that compares cached results against fresh extraction every 10% of requests, and if divergence is detected, escalate to a human reviewer
options.explanation: This catches errors after they happen (600+ records already wrong). Validation is reactive, not preventive. Escalation creates bottlenecks rather than automating invalidation.

questions.algorithmTrace: C wastes savings (anti-pattern). D is reactive validation without prevention. B is blind TTL without semantic awareness. A ties cache invalidation to structure changes with automatic version detection. Smart caching > blind TTL. COST-AWARE (keep 95% hits) > BRUTE-FORCE (purge everything). Select A.

questions.question: A CI/CD agent auto-remediates failed test suites by proposing code fixes and re-running tests. A developer manually applies fix #47 to the codebase; seconds later, the agent (unaware of the manual fix) proposes the same fix again and re-triggers a full test run. The test passes, but costs double ($40 instead of $20) and generates a duplicate commit in history. This happens 12-18 times per week across the 4 remediation agents. Recovery is only manual de-duplication. The team runs ~200 test remediation attempts per day. What approach provides exactly-once semantics without blocking concurrency?

options.label: A
options.text: Assign each proposed fix a deterministic idempotency key based on: (fix_rule_id + file_path + line_number + old_code_hash + new_code_hash). Before applying a fix, query a deduplication log: if the key exists and status='APPLIED', skip it; if status='IN_PROGRESS', wait; otherwise mark as 'IN_PROGRESS', apply, then commit and mark 'APPLIED'. Multiple agents can check-and-apply concurrently; the key prevents re-application.
options.explanation: This is idempotency + deduplication, the reliability pattern for exactly-once. Deterministic keys enable safe concurrency without lock-step orchestration. Check-before-apply is atomic. Concurrent agents can work independently; the key prevents duplicates. CODE > PROMPTS (programmatic dedup, not instructions).

options.label: B
options.text: Serialize all test remediation attempts through a single queue, processing them strictly sequentially so no two agents attempt fixes in parallel
options.explanation: This is valid but suboptimal. Serialization kills concurrency and throughput. With 200 attempts/day, serial processing adds unacceptable latency. Idempotent keys solve the same problem with full parallelism.

options.label: C
options.text: After each fix application, write the fix to a 'already_applied' log. Before proposing new fixes, the agent checks this log and skips any fix already recorded
options.explanation: This is partially valid but lacks determinism. The log is shared state with race conditions. If two agents check simultaneously, both might think they're the first to apply. No atomic check-and-mark.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement a human review step where a developer approves each fix before execution, preventing accidental duplicates
options.explanation: This is a business logic change, not a technical reliability pattern. It adds cost (human review) without solving the deduplication problem. You want automation, not human gates.

questions.algorithmTrace: D adds human overhead (not idempotency). C has race conditions (non-deterministic). B serializes and loses throughput (anti-pattern for 200 attempts/day). A uses deterministic idempotency keys with concurrent-safe check-and-mark. Idempotency is the pattern for exactly-once. CODE > PROMPTS. Select A.

questions.question: A code explanation agent serves 50-100 active developers per day, providing architectural summaries for complex modules. System logs show: P99 latency is 2.8 seconds, but during high load (40+ concurrent users), P99 jumps to 18 seconds. Direct availability is 94% during peak hours (6% of requests timeout). Memory usage spikes to 8.4GB during peaks. Response quality metrics show that when latency exceeds 8 seconds, explanation accuracy drops from 91% to 74% (likely token-limit cutting explanations short). You want to maximize availability and quality during peaks without unlimited cost scaling. What strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement progressive degradation with three quality tiers: Tier 1 (full context, 2-3 sec SLA) for <20 users; Tier 2 (core facts only, 1-2 sec SLA) for 20-60 users; Tier 3 (summary bullets, 0.5 sec SLA) for 60+ users. Route incoming requests based on current load. Monitor accuracy to ensure Tier 3 explanations still meet >75% accuracy threshold.
options.explanation: This is graceful degradation + COST-AWARE provisioning. You scale quality based on load rather than trying to serve everyone at full quality (which causes timeouts and failures). Progressive tiers prevent the cliff at 8 seconds. CODE > PROMPTS (programmatic routing).

options.label: B
options.text: Increase server resources (CPU, memory) to handle 100 concurrent users without degradation, ensuring all requests always receive full-quality explanations
options.explanation: This is BRUTE-FORCE. Unlimited scaling is expensive and ignores that response time is valuable. Paying for Tier 1 for everyone is cost-waste. 50% of users don't need 2.8 sec; they'd accept 1 sec bullets.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a request queue with user notifications ("wait 15 seconds for full explanation, or get quick summary now"). Let users choose quality vs. latency trade-off.
options.explanation: This adds friction and decision burden. Users don't want to choose; they want good UX. A programmatic strategy (A) handles the tradeoff automatically without asking. Also doesn't address the 94% availability issue.

options.label: D
options.text: Cache full explanations for all common modules and serve cached results instantly. Only generate new explanations for novel code patterns.
options.explanation: Caching helps but doesn't solve load-dependent degradation. During high load, even cache lookups can contend. This doesn't address the concurrency scaling problem.

questions.algorithmTrace: D helps but doesn't solve concurrency scaling. C adds user friction (explicit choice). B is brute-force cost-waste. A implements programmatic tier routing with SLAs per load level. Graceful degradation is the reliability pattern for load management. COST-AWARE > BRUTE-FORCE. Select A.

questions.question: A multi-turn code generation agent helps build a microservice across 18 chat exchanges. System metrics show: (1) Requests drop 8% if response time >4 seconds (user impatience); (2) Claude's 200K token context means average requests fit comfortably for exchanges 1-12, but turn 14+ have risk of truncation; (3) By exchange 16, token budget for new context is just 8-12K, which is insufficient for complex refactoring requests; (4) Restarting conversation loses architectural continuity and costs 15 extra exchanges to re-establish context. Historical data shows 23% of exchanges 15-18 involve clarifying questions about decisions made in exchanges 3-8. How should the system approach this constraint?

options.label: A
options.text: At exchange 12 (proactively, before truncation risk), extract and compact session state: (1) generated classes/functions, (2) architectural decisions, (3) open issues. Present this as a structured summary before exchange 13. If user's exchange 13 request references early details, inject the summary into the context window. If not, keep summary available but don't use tokens. This prevents both truncation AND re-clarification loops.
options.explanation: PROGRESSIVE CONTEXT LOADING. You proactively compact before the cliff, not after tokens are exhausted. Structured state is injected on-demand (COST-AWARE), not dumped upfront (BRUTE-FORCE). You prevent the 23% re-clarification cost. CODE > PROMPTS (programmatic injection, not hoping the model remembers).

options.label: B
options.text: At exchange 15, detect if remaining token budget is <15K, and automatically suggest the user start a new conversation with a full context handoff document
options.explanation: Restarting is a fallback, not a strategy. Users hate context switches. You're accepting the 15 extra exchanges overhead and the 8% request drop from long latencies. This doesn't prevent the problem.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement a sliding context window where the oldest exchanges (1-5) are discarded in favor of new exchanges, keeping only the most recent 10 exchanges in context
options.explanation: This directly causes the 23% re-clarification rate because users reference early decisions. Loss of architectural continuity makes refactoring unsafe.

options.label: D
options.text: Run all code generation requests through a preliminary cost estimator that predicts if they'll exceed 18 exchanges, and reject those requests upfront with a message to break them into smaller tasks
options.explanation: Rejecting work is giving up. Users have legitimate complex requests. This doesn't solve the problem; it limits the agent's usefulness.

questions.algorithmTrace: D rejects work (unacceptable). C loses critical context (triggers re-clarification). B falls back to restart (expensive and loses momentum). A proactively compacts at exchange 12 with on-demand injection. Progressive context loading, cost-aware activation, deterministic state tracking. Select A.
